Still, the tape screams mid-round steal (Round 2-4 projection)—a versatile, powerful anchor ready to plug in as a rotational-to-starter Day 1 force with the physical tools to grow into a dominant NFL nose tackle.

name: Ted Hurst
summary: Ted Hurst is a 6'3", 207-pound "X" receiver from Georgia State whose stock is surging following a dominant 1,004-yard senior season and a standout performance at the Senior Bowl. A true physical specimen who made Bruce Feldman’s "Freaks List," Hurst pair’s legitimate size with surprising athleticism, having recorded a 20.33 MPH max speed and a 10'8" broad jump. Scouts laud his massive catch radius and elite ability to finish through contact, evidenced by a 61% career contested-catch rate. While he lacks elite suddenness and faces questions about separation against top-tier corners, his vertical "gliding" speed and red-zone utility are undeniable. Currently projected as a Day 2 (Round 3) riser, Hurst offers the high-ceiling profile of a productive perimeter starter for teams seeking a physical, chain-moving target.

name: Malachi Lawrence
summary: Malachi Lawrence (6’4”, 255 lbs) flashes enticing edge traits, combining length, burst, and physicality while posting 6+ sacks and double-digit tackles for loss. He sets a firm edge against the run and shows closing speed when working in space. Lawrence’s pass-rush plan remains a work in progress, relying heavily on athleticism rather than sequencing. While not a Senior Bowl standout, his production and tools keep evaluators interested. He projects as a developmental edge rusher who can contribute early on passing downs, with starter potential if his technique and consistency continue trending upward.

name: Daylen Everette
summary: Daylen Everette (6’1/190) operates as a cerebral boundary corner who anchored Georgia’s elite secondary throughout their 2025 run. This First-Team All-SEC selection excels in press-man coverage, deploying "patient hands" to neutralize vertical threats. While he lacks elite, "twitchy" recovery burst, his diagnostic skills and 82.5 PFF coverage grade make him a scheme-diverse asset for any NFL system. Following a strong Senior Bowl showing where he erased top-tier receivers in one-on-ones, Everette has solidified a late-first-round grade. He possesses the pro-ready technique and poise required to start immediately for a contending defense.

name: Kyle Louis
summary: Kyle Louis (6’2/230) saw rotational linebacker snaps at Pittsburgh in 2025, contributing solid range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in diagnosing plays. Run defense, blitz packages, and coverage situations benefited from his sideline-to-sideline traits and pursuit motor. Developmental off-ball linebacker with three-down potential. Physicality and shedding remain inconsistent against climbing blockers; slow reads and false steps occur in aggressive run fits; added functional strength and mass are needed to anchor in the box. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth linebacker and special teams contributor if run-game technique improves and bulk is added.

name: Jaishawn Barham
summary: Jaishawn Barham (6’3/248) solidified his first-round trajectory after a dominant Senior Bowl week, where he showcased elite "stack-and-shed" technique during team drills. A violent, multi-role disruptor who transitioned to a hybrid edge role at Michigan, Barham’s diagnostic instincts and massive frame make him a scheme-proof asset for NFL front sevens. While his long-speed remains a talking point for vertical coverage, his ability to anchor the line and collapse pockets with linear power is unmatched in this class. A projected Day 1 selection, Barham is a high-floor "plug-and-play" defender with immediate All-Pro potential in 2026.

name: Tim Keenan III
summary: Tim Keenan III (6’3/305) rotated in Alabama’s interior snaps in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure in run and pass phases. Quickness for size, active hands to disengage, and power to anchor against doubles stood out when pad level stayed low. Developmental 3-technique/nose tackle with multi-alignment value and high motor in short bursts. Leverage issues arise when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters are limited with little bend against quicker guards; stamina drops in extended reps. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational depth if technique is refined and strength is added.

name: Jalon Kilgore
summary: Jalon Kilgore (6’1/211) functions as a violent, pro-ready "chess piece" who bolstered his stock after a first-team All-SEC campaign at South Carolina. Primarily a nickel defender with 1,382 career snaps in the slot, Kilgore combines elite explosive traits with a 4.5-speed profile that allows him to "crash" run lanes or neutralize modern tight ends. While scouts noted his 11.4% missed-tackle rate as an area for refinement, his nine career interceptions and "alpha" leadership—voted team captain by peers—shined during Senior Bowl week. Currently projected as a Day 2 value with "safety-nickel" hybrid utility, Kilgore offers the high-end processing and physical play-strength required to stabilize an NFL sub-package immediately in 2026.

name: Skyler Bell
summary: Skyler Bell (6’0/190) emerged as a "statistical monster" in 2025, leading UConn with 101 receptions and 1,278 yards to become a Biletnikoff Award finalist. This former Wisconsin transfer combines elite route-running savvy with the "suddenness" required to manipulate stems and keep defenders off-balance. While he lacks the "freakish" vertical burners of a top-ten pick, his 13 touchdowns and ability to execute sharp, well-defined cuts at all three levels of the field impressed evaluators in Mobile. After a "perfect" Senior Bowl where he was lauded for his high-IQ ability to exploit defensive weaknesses, Bell is trending toward a mid-Day 2 selection. He possesses the veteran "cool" and reliable hands needed to become a primary NFL chain-mover.

name: Joe Royer
summary: Joe Royer (6’5”, 250 lbs) brings NFL size with legitimate receiving polish after producing 50+ catches and 500+ yards in his final season. He was one of the more reliable tight ends during Senior Bowl week, consistently winning one-on-ones with route tempo and strong hands. Royer excels attacking zone coverage, finishing through contact, and working the seams, though he’s more smooth than explosive after the catch. Blocking effort is solid but not dominant. With steady production and an effective Senior Bowl showing, he profiles as a Day 2 tight end who can contribute early as a pass-game option.

name: Oscar Delp
summary: Oscar Delp (6’4/245) started at tight end for Georgia in 2025, contributing receptions in seam and red-zone roles alongside blocking effort on over 50% of snaps. Route nuance, leaping at the catch point, and versatility in-line/slot alignments appeared. Developmental move tight end with receiving traits and every-down value. Blocking technique and anchor remain inconsistent against power edges; height advantage is limited in contested situations; vertical speed to stretch seams is modest against safety help. Day 3 projection (Round 4-6) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational TE2/TE3 if blocking hand placement is refined and strength is added.

name: Cade Klubnik
summary: Cade Klubnik (6’2/210) started at quarterback for Clemson in 2025, completing over 65% of passes for 3,000+ yards and 25+ touchdowns while adding rushing production. Arm strength, quick release, pocket poise, mobility to extend plays, and accuracy under pressure stood out. Dual-threat quarterback capable of attacking all levels and leading the offense effectively. Happy feet and hold-the-ball tendencies surface against pressure; aggressive decisions occur in tight windows; pre-snap processing can lag versus complex blitzes. Mid-to-late Day 2 projection (Round 3-5) in the 2026 NFL Draft with backup-to-starter potential in schemes that leverage arm talent and mobility.

name: Demond Claiborne
summary: Demond Claiborne (5’10/195) serves as a "shifty" all-purpose weapon who left Wake Forest as the program’s fourth all-time leading rusher. Recording 907 yards and 10 touchdowns in 2025, Claiborne excels in space, using elite short-area burst to turn check-downs into chunk plays. While his diminutive frame raises durability concerns for a full-time NFL workload, his 1,000+ all-purpose yards in consecutive seasons highlight his consistency. After showing out as a return specialist during the Shrine Bowl, Claiborne projects as a late-Day 3 value. He is a scheme-diverse "change-of-pace" back with game-breaking return potential. Home run hitter.

name: Tyreak Sapp
summary: Tyreak Sapp (6’5/265) dominated Florida’s edge in 2025—servings a relentless, high-motor disruptor that offenses dreaded lining up against, turning into a nightmare 5-technique/EDGE hybrid who consistently wrecked plays with explosive get-off, violent hand usage, and gap-penetrating power that erased running lanes and collapsed pockets. Posting 8 sacks, 12 TFLs, and elite run-defense grades, Sapp earned All-SEC honorable mention and surged into mid-round prospect status (EDGE15-20 consensus range) as one of the most productive and athletic big men on the edge in the SEC. Long levers that create leverage nightmares for tackles, sudden first-step burst, and a nonstop motor that wears down blockers over four quarters—he’s a versatile force capable of anchoring the edge, rushing from wide alignments, or even sliding inside on sub-packages. High-motor traits and physical tools scream rotational-to-starter upside with the potential to climb boards if he refines his rush plan. Weaknesses: Average bend and corner speed limit his ability to win with pure speed against quicker tackles; pass-rush counters remain raw and one-dimensional; occasional high pad level can be exploited by savvy linemen. Still, the tape and production make him a high-ceiling Day 2 steal in the 2026 draft—Round 3-5 projection as a powerful, disruptive rotational piece ready to contribute from Week 1 with starter potential in the right scheme.

name: Harold Perkins Jr.
summary: Harold Perkins (6’1/235) remains a generational athletic specimen who returned to LSU in 2025 following a 2024 ACL injury. Shifting to a "Star" hybrid role, Perkins regained his 4.4-speed and "game-wrecking" burst, recording 8.0 sacks and 14.0 TFLs. While his thin frame raises concerns regarding his ability to stack-and-shed interior linemen, his elite pursuit range and bend off the edge make him a defensive coordinator’s dream. Currently projected as a mid-first-round pick, Perkins offers rare versatility to rush, cover, or spy, making him a franchise-altering asset for multiple NFL defensive fronts.

name: Mike Washington Jr.
summary: Mike Washington Jr. (6’2/225) represents a "breakout" physical specimen who recorded 1,070 yards and 9 touchdowns for Arkansas in 2025. This bigger-bodied back combines elite vision with a "Rhamondre Stevenson-style" ability to make defenders miss despite his imposing frame. While fumbles have been a developmental hurdle, his career-high 6.4 yards per carry and productive receiving floor highlight true three-down potential. After impressing in Mobile with his quick-decision making and "fall-forward" power, Washington projects as a fourth-round gem. He offers the functional mass and motor to handle a heavy professional workload from Week 1.

name: Justin Joly
summary: Justin Joly (6’3/235) represents the "technician’s ideal" at tight end, having utilized his 2025 transfer to NC State to showcase elite "Big Slot" utility. This former UConn standout excels in contested scenarios, utilizing 4.58-speed and a "basketball-style" catch radius to snag 11 of 13 deep-third targets during his breakout season. While his in-line anchor strength remains a developmental hurdle for heavy-set run schemes, his 12 forced missed tackles and 661-yard receiving floor highlight a "game-breaking" ceiling. Currently vaulting into early Day 2 consideration, Joly offers the rare vertical twitch and route-running savvy required to become a primary safety-valve for an NFL quarterback. He is the class’s premier move-TE prospect with immediate high-leverage rotational value.

name: Bud Clark
summary: Bud Clark (6’1/205 est.) contributed in TCU’s secondary in 2025, showing range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run support and coverage. Box/single-high traits and pursuit motor appeared in rotational snaps. Developmental safety with depth upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; physicality in run support needs refinement; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth safety or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are polished.

name: Josh Cameron
summary: Josh Cameron (6’1”, 205 lbs) produced as a dependable perimeter receiver, recording 700+ yards and multiple touchdowns while serving as a go-to option on intermediate routes. He plays with strong hands, good body control, and toughness over the middle, consistently finishing through contact. Cameron isn’t a burner, but he creates separation with route nuance and timing. While lacking major awards or Senior Bowl exposure, his steady production stands out. He projects as a depth wide receiver who can contribute on third downs and special teams, with upside as a reliable WR3 or WR4.

name: Michael Taaffe
summary: Michael Taaffe (6’0/190) represents a high-IQ "football junkie" who evolved from a walk-on into an All-SEC First Team leader at Texas. The 2025 Wuerffel Trophy winner combines elite diagnostic skills with a "nose for the football," recording 70 tackles and crucial interceptions. While he lacks the "freakish" athletic profile of top-tier safeties, his 4.52-speed and reliable tackling make him a "plug-and-play" asset. After a productive Shrine Bowl where he showcased elite communication skills, Taaffe offers third-round value with the high leadership and special teams floor needed to stabilize an NFL locker room.

name: Kevin Coleman Jr.
summary: Kevin Coleman Jr. (5’11/185) worked primarily in Missouri’s slot in 2025, displaying quick separation underneath, reliable hands, and yards after catch on slants/screens. Route nuance, acceleration out of breaks, and space creation appeared in limited production. Slot traits and return potential provide developmental upside. Size and contested-catch ability limit him against physical corners; deep-threat profile remains modest; press coverage at the line creates inconsistency. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational slot piece or special teams contributor if releases are refined and strength is added.

name: Domani Jackson
summary: Domani Jackson (6’1”, 195 lbs) plays with confidence and competitiveness, allowing limited separation while contributing multiple pass breakups and interceptions over the season. He’s fluid in man coverage, comfortable in off-zone, and willing in run support. Jackson’s recruiting pedigree shows in his athletic traits, though he can be stressed by elite vertical speed if technique slips. While not a Senior Bowl participant, he earned All-Conference recognition and steady draft buzz. His profile fits a versatile defensive scheme, projecting as a potential Day 2 or early Day 3 corner with starter upside.

name: Sam Roush
summary: Sam Roush (6’5/250) stands out as the premiere "pure blocker" of the 2026 class, earning Lance Zierlein’s praise for his elite "drive-blocking" technique at Stanford. This physical, high-IQ tight end provides a "soothing" presence for quarterbacks, serving as a reliable safety valve with over 117 career catches despite inconsistent offensive play. While he lacks elite vertical burners to stretch the deep third, his ability to displace defenders in the run game and secure difficult catches in traffic is unmatched. Roush profiles as a high-value mid-round pick with immediate Super Bowl-contributing utility.

name: Zxavian Harris
summary: Zxavian Harris (6’3/305) contributed in Mississippi State’s interior rotation during 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure across limited snaps. Quickness off the snap for his frame, active hands to disengage, and power to hold against doubles stood out when pad level remained low. Multi-alignment value (3-technique to nose) and short-burst motor offer developmental upside. Leverage issues appear when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters stay limited with little bend vs. quicker guards; stamina drops in longer series. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft as rotational depth with potential for snaps if technique and strength improve.

name: Devin Moore
summary: Devin Moore (5’11/185 est.) contributed in Florida’s secondary rotation in 2025, showing quick feet, competitive ball skills, and solid run support in nickel and boundary snaps. Press technique at the line, mirroring underneath, and instincts in zone/match coverage appeared in limited production. Developmental corner with nickel/slot upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent; technique lapses occur during transitions. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth corner or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are refined.

name: Zane Durant
summary: Zane Durant (6’3/305 est.) rotated in Penn State’s interior snaps in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure in run and pass phases. Quickness for size, active hands to disengage, and power to anchor against doubles stood out when pad level stayed low. Developmental 3-technique/nose tackle with multi-alignment value and high motor in short bursts. Leverage issues arise when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters are limited with little bend against quicker guards; stamina drops in extended reps. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational depth if technique is refined and strength is added.

name: CJ  Daniels
summary: C.J. Daniels (6’2”, 195 lbs) wins vertically, leveraging speed and ball tracking to generate chunk plays downfield. He posted 700+ receiving yards and multiple explosive touchdowns, routinely stretching coverage and winning contested catches along the boundary. Daniels flashes separation late in routes and shows confidence attacking the football, though physical press corners can disrupt his timing early. He lacks consistent yards-after-catch creation but profiles as a field-stretcher defenses must account for. While not a major award winner, his production and athletic profile give him mid-round appeal as a rotational outside receiver with upside in a vertical passing offense.

name: Treydan Stukes
summary: Treydan Stukes (6’2/200) represents a "shutdown" secondary artist who utilized his 2025 senior season at Arizona to earn AP Third Team All-American honors. This long-limbed technician excels in press-man coverage, utilizing "patient hands" and elite recovery speed to record four interceptions and six pass breakups this past season. While a medical redshirt in 2024 raised durability questions, his leadership in a secondary that ranked second nationally in interceptions proved his "alpha" mentality. Currently projected as a fringe Day 1 or early Day 2 value, Stukes offers the rare combination of height, diagnostic instincts, and ball-tracking skills needed to neutralize physical NFL targets. He is a scheme-diverse corner with the technique to start immediately in Week 1.

name: Brenen Thompson
summary: Brenen Thompson (6’2/195 est.) contributed as a rotational wide receiver for Mississippi State in 2025, showing quick separation, reliable hands, and yards after catch on intermediate routes. Route nuance and body control appeared in limited production. Developmental slot/outside receiver with dependable traits. Size aids contested-catch situations; production remains modest; press coverage at the line creates inconsistency. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth piece or special teams contributor if releases are refined and strength is added.

name: Gracen Halton
summary: Gracen Halton (6’2”, 305 lbs) provides interior versatility, aligning across the defensive front while finishing with tackles for loss and pocket disruption that doesn’t always show in raw sack totals. He plays with good leverage, quick hands, and awareness on stunts, though his anchor can be tested by double teams. Halton’s motor and football IQ stand out more than pure explosiveness. He projects as a Round 5–6 pick, offering early snaps as a rotational interior defensive lineman, with upside in sub-packages that emphasize movement and penetration.

name: Caden Curry
summary: Caden Curry (6’3/250 est.) rotated on Ohio State’s edge in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pressures with quick first step and power bull rush. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in pass-rush and run-defense snaps. Developmental edge with starter traits if strength and technique improve. Bend and corner speed remain average vs. quicker tackles; pass-rush plan is power-heavy with limited counters; leverage issues occur when pad level rises. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational disruptor in schemes that value motor and power.

name: Hezekiah Masses
summary: Hezekiah Masses (5’11/185) appeared in California’s nickel and boundary packages in 2025, showing quick feet, competitive ball skills, and solid run support in rotational reps. Press technique at the line, mirroring underneath, and instincts in zone/match coverage showed promise. Athletic profile and competitive traits fit as a developmental corner with nickel/slot upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent; technique lapses occur during coverage transitions. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth corner or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are refined.

name: Drew Allar
summary: Drew Allar (6’5”, 240 lbs) flashes every trait NFL teams look for in a prototype pocket passer, backed by 3,000+ passing yards and a strong touchdown-to-interception ratio. His arm strength jumps off the tape, particularly on deep outs and intermediate throws over the middle. Allar shows improved command of protections and progressions, earning All-Conference consideration, though pressure still speeds him up at times. Mobility is functional but not a featured part of his game. Teams will bet on tools, size, and trajectory, viewing him as a high-upside quarterback who fits best in a timing-based system.

name: Tacario Davis
summary: Tacario Davis (6’4”, 195 lbs) turned heads throughout the pre-draft process, standing out at the Senior Bowl with his length, physicality, and ball disruption. He recorded multiple interceptions and double-digit pass breakups, using his reach to erase throwing windows in press and zone looks. Davis excels rerouting receivers but can struggle transitioning against quick cuts. Tackling effort is evident, though angles need refinement. His rare size-speed profile gives him strong draft appeal, projecting as a press-capable outside corner with clear starter upside in length-driven defensive schemes.

name: Skyler Gill-Howard
summary: Skyler Gill-Howard brings a competitive, assignment-sound profile that NFL teams tend to value more than the box score alone. He plays with good processing speed, disciplined eyes, and consistent effort snap to snap, allowing him to stay within structure and limit explosive breakdowns. Gill-Howard doesn’t rely on elite traits, but his reliability, toughness, and football IQ show up on tape, particularly in situational and complementary roles. He projects as a Day 3 selection (Rounds 5–7) or priority free agent. Early in his career, he profiles as a special teams contributor and rotational depth piece, with the upside to earn defensive snaps as trust and reps accumulate.

name: Eli Raridon
summary: Eli Raridon (6’7/250) solidified his status as a "quarterback-friendly" weapon after starting all 12 games for Notre Dame in 2025 following a grueling ACL recovery. This prototypical "Y" prospect utilizes his massive frame and "above-the-rim" body control to record 482 receiving yards and a career-best 15.1 yards-per-catch average. While scouts initially questioned his lateral twitch, his "dominant" performance against NC State—recording 109 yards on seven catches—proved he possesses the explosive traits to dominate the intermediate seam. Currently projected as a late Day 2 value, Raridon offers a rare blend of in-line blocking prowess and vertical-receiving upside. He is the quintessential pro-ready tight end with the mass and motor to start immediately in Week 1.

name: Chris McClellan
summary: Chris McClellon (6’3/290 est.) lined up in Missouri’s interior rotation in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure in run and pass phases. Quickness for size, active hands, and power to anchor doubles stood out when pad level stayed low. Developmental 3-technique/nose tackle with multi-alignment value and short-burst motor. Leverage losses occur when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters limited with little bend vs quicker guards; stamina drops in extended reps. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft—rotational depth if technique refines and strength increases.

name: Eric Rivers
summary: Eric Rivers (5’11”, 190 lbs) emerged as a dynamic slot weapon, leading his offense with 80+ receptions and over 1,000 receiving yards, consistently creating separation underneath and after the catch. Rivers excels with short-area quickness, route tempo, and feel versus zone coverage, making him a reliable chain mover. He adds value on jet motion and screens, averaging strong yards after contact for his size. While not a Senior Bowl participant, his production earned All-Conference honors. Limited catch radius and play strength cap his ceiling, but he projects as a high-floor slot receiver with early rotational value.

name: Dallen Bentley
summary: Dallen Bentley (6’5/240 est.) contributed as a rotational tight end for Utah in 2025, showing receptions in seam roles, good hands, and blocking effort on short-yardage and run plays. Route nuance and leaping at the catch point appeared in limited snaps. Developmental in-line/move tight end with receiving traits and every-down value. Blocking technique and anchor remain inconsistent against power edges; height advantage is limited in contested situations; vertical speed to stretch seams is modest. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational TE2/TE3 if blocking hand placement is refined and strength is added.

name: Dae'Quan Wright
summary: Dae'Quan Wright (6’4/255) operates as a "big-bodied" vertical threat who capped a productive Ole Miss career with 635 yards and 5 touchdowns in 2025. This former basketball standout utilizes his 11.1-speed and elite body control to dominate the intermediate "seam" and create mismatches against slower linebackers. While scouts noted his need for improved in-line anchor strength during Senior Bowl week, his natural hands and ability to turn routine check-downs into chunk plays solidify a Day 2 grade. Wright offers the high-upside "Big Slot" utility required for modern, pass-heavy NFL offenses.

name: Reggie Virgil
summary: Reggie Virgil (6’3”, 200 lbs) brings boundary size and vertical ability to the receiver position, producing 600+ yards and multiple touchdowns while consistently stretching coverage. He wins with length, body control, and ball tracking, particularly on deep sideline throws. Separation against tight man coverage remains inconsistent, and his route tree needs refinement. Without major postseason exposure, he remains a projection-based evaluation. Virgil profiles as a Round 6–7 selection, contributing early as a depth outside receiver and special teams option, with upside as a situational deep threat.

name: Rayshaun Benny
summary: Rayshaun Benny (6’4/305) stands as a "Day 1 impact" defensive tackle who anchored Michigan’s dominant 2025 front with a Third-Team All-Big Ten nod. This Detroit native combines brute interior strength with surprisingly nimble footwork, proving his effectiveness as both a run-stuffer and gap-penetrator. After a "dominant" Senior Bowl performance that Dane Brugler suggests puts him in the First-Round conversation, Benny’s stock is at an all-time high. He possesses the heavy hands and "pro-ready" anchor required to neutralize NFL double-teams. Benny is the premiere interior defensive cornerstone of the 2026 draft cycle.

name: Louis Moore
summary: Louis Moore (6’0”, 190 lbs) brings physicality and competitiveness to the secondary, producing 70+ tackles, double-digit pass breakups, and multiple interceptions. He plays with confidence in man coverage and isn’t shy about triggering downhill in run support. Moore’s instincts and toughness stand out, though he can be tested vertically by elite speed. He flashed well in postseason practices, boosting his stock late in the cycle. Without major awards, his appeal comes from reliability and edge. He projects as a Day 3 defensive back with special teams value and potential as a nickel or depth safety.

name: Aaron Anderson
summary: Aaron Anderson (5’8/188) represents a "lightning-fast" gadget weapon who declared for the 2026 draft following a dynamic, albeit injury-shortened, 2025 campaign at LSU. Combining elite 4.37-speed with the vision to erase pursuit angles in space, Anderson functions as a primary separator who thrived in the slot with 33 receptions this past season. While his diminutive size limits his boundary utility and red-zone "box-out" potential, his 884-yard breakout in 2024 highlights a "game-breaking" WR1 ceiling. Currently trending as a Round 4 developmental riser, Anderson offers the "Tyreek Hill-esque" acceleration and return-game utility needed to transform a creative NFL offensive system. He is a premier "one-cut" playmaker with the rare twitch to turn routine screens into house-calls.

name: Le'Veon Moss
summary: Le'Veon Moss (5’11/210) remains a "violence-first" runner who overcame significant injuries to lead Texas A&M’s ground game in 2025. This compact, low-center-of-gravity back excels in gap schemes, utilizing elite contact balance and "piston-like" leg drive to maximize yards after contact. While scouts question his top-end long speed following his 2024 ACL tear, his 86% touch-or-target rate when on the field highlights his "workhorse" reliability. Following a strong Senior Bowl where he showcased improved pass-blocking poise, Moss has solidified a second-round grade. He is the quintessential "finisher" for a physical NFL offense.

name: Josh Cuevas
summary: Josh Cuevas (6’3”, 250 lbs) offers intriguing upside at tight end, combining size with movement skills while totaling 30+ receptions and multiple touchdowns. He flashes soft hands and comfort working up the seam, particularly in play-action concepts. Cuevas remains inconsistent as a blocker and needs refinement with route detail, but the athletic profile is appealing. Without major postseason exposure, he remains slightly under the radar. He profiles as a Round 5–6 pick, projecting initially as a TE2/TE3 and red-zone option, with upside if his blocking and consistency develop.

name: Bryce Lance
summary: Bryce Lance (6’3”, 215 lbs) operates as a smooth, possession-oriented wide receiver who consistently moves the chains, finishing with 60+ receptions and 800+ yards. He wins with route detail, body control, and strong hands in traffic, particularly on intermediate patterns. Lance lacks top-end speed, limiting his vertical threat, but he compensates with timing and physicality. He earned Second-Team All-Conference honors and served as a quarterback-friendly target throughout the season. His profile fits a reliable WR3/WR4 role at the next level, offering early contribution in structured passing attacks.

name: Mikail Kamara
summary: Mikail Kamara (6’1/265) anchors Indiana’s historic 2025 defensive front as a "high-motor" disruptor. While his 2025 sack totals dipped, his 58 total pressures led the Big Ten, showcasing a relentless ability to collapse the pocket. Scouts noted his "violent" hand-usage and leverage-based power during a standout Senior Bowl week, where he consistently won against larger tackles. Despite being "undersized" for a traditional 5-technique, his diagnostic skills and "non-stop" pursuit solidify a second-round grade. Kamara provides high-floor, rotational edge-rusher value with the functional strength to set the edge and impact run games immediately.

name: Keyron Crawford
summary: Keyron Crawford (6’4/255) emerges as a "fast-riser" who spearheaded Auburn’s pass rush with 43 pressures and a formidable "swipe-and-chop" move. This bendy, high-IQ athlete excels at dipping under tackles to reach the quarterback, showcasing the lateral agility needed for a 3-4 outside linebacker role. While he occasionally struggles to hold the point of attack against heavier "power" blockers, his elite quickness and hand-speed were the talk of Senior Bowl one-on-ones. Projected as a third-round selection, Crawford possesses the rare athletic ceiling and "twitch" to develop into a consistent double-digit sack artist.

name: TJ Hall
summary: TJ Hall (6’2”, 200 lbs) offers intriguing size-speed traits at cornerback, pairing length with competitive coverage skills. He finished the season with multiple interceptions and double-digit pass breakups, showing comfort in press-man and zone-match looks. Hall tracks the ball well and plays with confidence at the catch point, though his footwork can get loose against sharp route runners. He wasn’t a Senior Bowl standout, but his production earned All-Conference mention. With refinement in technique and consistency, Hall projects as a developmental outside corner with starting upside in man-heavy schemes.

name: Lander Barton
summary: Lander Barton (6’4”, 235 lbs) offers versatility as a modern linebacker, combining length with range to post 80+ tackles, sacks, and pass breakups. He moves fluidly in space, showing comfort dropping into coverage while still contributing as a blitzer. Barton’s play strength remains a developmental area, particularly when taking on blocks inside. He earned All-Conference recognition and flashed during postseason evaluations. His skill set fits today’s sub-package-heavy NFL, projecting as a hybrid linebacker who can contribute on passing downs early and grow into a three-down role with added strength.

name: DeMonte Capehart
summary: DeMonte Capehart (6’4”, 315 lbs) operates as a power interior presence capable of anchoring against the run and collapsing pockets. He logged 30+ tackles with several tackles for loss, showing flashes of dominance when his pad level stays low. Capehart generates push with raw strength but remains inconsistent finishing plays as a pass rusher. He doesn’t rack up sacks, yet his impact shows up in muddy pockets and disrupted run lanes. Without major postseason accolades, his value lies in projection. He profiles as an early-down rotational defensive tackle with upside if his hand usage and counters improve.

name: Thaddeus Dixon
summary: Thaddeus Dixon (6’1/195) operates as a "sticky" man-coverage specialist who recorded 10 pass deflections and high-end PFF grades for North Carolina in 2025. This long-limbed boundary corner excels at pattern-matching, using his 22 career PBUs to highlight elite ball-tracking and diagnostic skills. While he can be overly aggressive, leading to occasional vertical lapses, his "violent" jams at the line and competitive "alpha" mentality impressed scouts in Mobile. Projected as a mid-round selection, Dixon offers the rare frame and recovery speed to neutralize physical NFL targets. He is a high-floor corner prospect.

name: Nick Singleton
summary: Nick Singleton (6’0/226) stands out as a "freak" athlete who combines 4.35-speed with elite play-strength, evidenced by his 655-pound squat. While he split carries in Penn State’s 2025 offense, Singleton remained a home-run threat, scoring 13 touchdowns and showcasing improved pass-blocking technique. Scouts note he can be "too patient" behind the line, but his ability to run through arm-tackles and erase pursuit angles makes him a second-round gem. After a successful draft-cycle showcasing his receiving floor, Singleton profiles as a "Jonathan Taylor-style" workhorse with the physical tools to headline a pro backfield.

name: Nadame Tucker
summary: Nadame Tucker (6’3/250) provides elite pass-rush efficiency, having recorded 14 sacks and a staggering 93.3 PFF grade for Western Michigan in 2025. This relentless edge-penetrator utilizes "violent" hand-usage and a sophisticated array of counters to consistently harass quarterbacks. While his "one-year wonder" status and older age (turning 26) have sparked some draft-board debate, his dominant Senior Bowl week silenced critics regarding his level of competition. Currently vaulting into Day 2 consideration, Tucker offers the high-motor, disruptive traits required for an immediate situational pass-rush role. He is the class’s premier sleeper edge-rusher.

name: Tanner Koziol
summary: Tanner Koziol (6’7”, 255 lbs) presents a massive catch radius at tight end, producing 40+ receptions, 500+ yards, and strong red-zone efficiency. He wins with size, body positioning, and hands, consistently boxing out defenders on contested targets. Koziol isn’t sudden and won’t separate vertically, but he understands leverage and spacing. Blocking effort is solid, though leverage can be an issue. He projects as a Round 4–5 selection, with early contribution as a red-zone package tight end and TE2, offering immediate situational value.

name: Marlin Klein
summary: Marlin Klein (6’6/260) rotated on Michigan’s edge in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pressures with length and power to convert speed to bull rush. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in pass-rush and run-defense snaps. Developmental edge with starter traits if bend and technique improve. Corner speed and bend remain average vs. quicker tackles; pass-rush counters are limited; leverage issues occur when pad level rises against stronger blockers. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational disruptor in schemes that value length and power.

name: Aiden Fisher
summary: Aiden Fisher (6’1/231) stands as a "generational" tackling machine who spearheaded Indiana’s historic 2025 run with 97 total tackles and 10.5 TFLs. This first-team All-American combines elite diagnostic skills with a "nose for the football," recording 13 tackles against Oregon and multiple double-digit performances throughout his senior year. While scouts question his "elite" vertical speed to cover NFL-caliber burners, his 1.5-sack utility and ability to navigate heavy traffic highlight a "pro-ready" middle-field floor. Currently vaulting into late-first or early-second-round consideration, Fisher offers the rare communication skills and "quarterback-friendly" leadership required to anchor a modern defense. He is a high-IQ defensive general with the motor to handle 100+ snaps a game.

name: Max Llewellyn
summary: Max Llewellyn (6’5/260 est.) rotated on Iowa’s edge in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pressures with good length and power to convert speed to bull rush. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in pass-rush and run-defense snaps. Developmental edge with starter traits if bend and technique improve. Corner speed and bend remain average vs. quicker tackles; pass-rush counters are limited; leverage issues occur when pad level rises against stronger blockers. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational disruptor in schemes that value length and power.

name: J'Mari Taylor
summary: J’Mari Taylor (5’10”, 175 lbs) brings explosive speed and big-play ability, averaging over 17 yards per catch with multiple long touchdowns. He threatens defenses vertically and horizontally, showing dangerous acceleration with the ball in his hands. Taylor’s size limits his effectiveness in contested situations, and physical coverage can disrupt his routes. While he lacks postseason accolades, his production and athletic profile stand out. NFL teams will view him as a developmental deep threat and gadget weapon who can contribute on special teams and manufactured touches while expanding his role offensively.

name: Bryce Boettcher
summary: Bryce Boettcher (6’2/230 est.) rotated at linebacker for Oregon in 2025, contributing solid range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run defense and coverage. Sideline-to-sideline traits and pursuit motor appeared in limited snaps. Developmental off-ball linebacker with three-down potential. Physicality and shedding remain inconsistent against climbing blockers; slow reads and false steps occur in aggressive run fits; added strength and mass are needed to anchor in the box. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth linebacker and special teams contributor if run-game technique improves.

name: Sawyer Robertson
summary: Sawyer Robertson (6’4”, 225 lbs) shows functional athleticism and toughness at quarterback, operating comfortably in RPO and movement-based concepts while accounting for 2,500+ total yards and 20+ touchdowns. He throws with decent velocity on short and intermediate routes and isn’t afraid to extend plays with his legs. Processing speed can waver when the first read is covered, and arm talent is solid rather than special. Robertson projects as a Round 6–7 pick or priority free agent, offering early value as a QB3 and scout-team operator, with upside as a developmental backup in a spread-heavy system.

name: Roman Hemby
summary: Roman Hemby (6’0/210) remains a "violence-first" lead back who utilized Indiana’s perfect 2025 season to record a career-high 1,120 rushing yards. This compact, four-year starter excels in gap schemes, utilizing elite "straightforward" vision and piston-like leg drive to maximize yards after contact. While he lacks elite home-run gear, his 710 career touches with only two fumbles highlight "scary" ball security and pro-ready reliability. Following a strong Senior Bowl showcasing improved pass-blocking poise, Hemby has solidified a third-round grade. He is the quintessential "finisher" for any physical NFL offense seeking a three-down floor.

name: John Michael Gyllenborg
summary: John Michael Gyllenborg (6’5/240) contributed as a rotational tight end for Wyoming in 2025, showing receptions in seam roles, good hands, and blocking effort on short-yardage plays. Route nuance and leaping at the catch point appeared in limited snaps. Developmental in-line/move tight end with receiving traits. Blocking technique and anchor remain inconsistent against power edges; height advantage is limited in contested situations; vertical speed to stretch seams is modest. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational TE2/TE3 if blocking hand placement is refined and strength is added.

name: Seth McGowan
summary: Seth McGowan (5’11”, 215 lbs) runs with purpose, piling up 800+ rushing yards and multiple touchdowns as a physical, between-the-tackles presence. He shows good vision, contact balance, and decisiveness hitting downhill lanes, particularly in short-yardage situations. McGowan doesn’t bring home-run speed, but he consistently falls forward and wears down defenses. Receiving production is modest, and pass protection remains uneven. Without postseason accolades, his appeal is role-based. He profiles as a dependable rotational NFL back capable of handling early-down work and situational carries in a committee approach.

name: Albert Regis
summary: Albert Regis (6’2/315) functions as a "violent" interior presence who declared for the 2026 NFL Draft after anchoring Texas A&M’s defensive line with a career-high 49 tackles. This graduate student combines brute strength with surprisingly nimble footwork, proving his effectiveness as a "forklift" run-stuffer who can also record multiple pass breakups at the line of scrimmage. While he isn't a "finesse" pass-rusher, his ability to consistently command double-teams and erase interior running lanes made him an SEC Defensive Lineman of the Week in 2025. Projected as a mid-round value, Regis offers the "dirty work" mentality and functional mass needed to stabilize an NFL defensive front immediately. He is the quintessential gap-eating anchor for any pro-style system.

name: Eric McAlister
summary: Eric McAlister (6’3/205) functions as an "explosive" deep-threat who spearheaded TCU’s vertical attack with a 1,000-yard breakout campaign in 2025. This Biletnikoff finalist combines long, gazelle-like strides with elite 18.2 yards-per-catch utility, consistently out-muscling corners in high-leverage contested situations. While his lean frame raises durability questions against physical NFL press, his 7.9 YAC-per-reception average highlights a "scary" open-field ceiling. Currently vaulting into early Day 2 consideration, McAlister offers the rare size-speed profile and "catch-to-attack" mentality required for a primary boundary role. He is a premier vertical weapon for the 2026 draft.

name: Taylen Green
summary: Taylen Green (6’3/210 est.) served as Arkansas’ backup quarterback in 2025, completing passes with good arm strength, quick release, and mobility to extend plays in limited action. Dual-threat traits and accuracy on timing routes appeared in spot duty. Developmental quarterback with backup potential. Decision-making under pressure and pre-snap processing remain inconsistent; happy feet and hold-the-ball tendencies surface against blitzes; arm talent is solid but not elite. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as developmental backup if mechanics and reads are refined.

name: Lewis Bond
summary: Lewis Bond (5’11/200) stands out as the premiere "chain-mover" of the 2026 class, serving as a high-IQ safety valve for Boston College throughout his five-year tenure. This "Z-Flowers-style" technician excels at tempoing his routes to manipulate defensive backs, utilizing elite short-area burst to turn shallow crossers into chunk gains. While he lacks the "explosive" ankle-breaking twitch of a top-tier separator, his reliable hands and "professional" approach to pattern-matching solidify a mid-round grade. Bond offers the high-floor, pro-ready reliability needed to stabilize an NFL receiving corps. He is a quarterback’s best friend.

name: Cole Payton
summary: Cole Payton (6’2/230) emerged as the Senior Bowl's "biggest FCS riser," silencing critics with a 193.8 efficiency rating and record-breaking total offense at North Dakota State. This sturdy, left-handed signal-caller operates with a "punishing" run-style and above-average velocity, showcasing the arm talent to drive the ball into intermediate NFL windows. While his big, three-quarter wind-up remains a developmental hurdle for congested pockets, his "Walter Payton-style" toughness and improved field-scanning ability solidify a second-round grade. Payton possesses the raw physical tools and high-IQ leadership required to become a franchise-level NFL protector.

name: Jaydn Ott
summary: Jaydn Ott is a polarizing "home run" threat whose draft stock relies heavily on his elite Cal tape. At his peak in 2023, he was a First-Team All-Pac-12 selection, racking up 1,315 rushing yards and showcasing rare acceleration with a gliding stride that made pursuit defenders look "silly." His Cal career featured legendary performances, including a 274-yard explosion against Arizona and a 100-yard kickoff return, cementing his reputation as a dynamic, three-down weapon. However, lingering injuries and a lack of usage during a disappointing 2025 season at Oklahoma—where he managed just 68 rushing yards—have muddied his projection. Currently viewed as a Day 3 flyer (late 4th-5th round), Ott must use the Combine to prove his 2023 explosiveness remains intact.

name: Bishop Fitzgerald
summary: Bishop Fitzgerald (6’3”, 315 lbs) profiles as a wide-bodied interior lineman built to control space and eat snaps, finishing the year with 30+ tackles and multiple tackles for loss. He plays with heavy hands, good anchor strength, and consistently holds ground against double teams. Fitzgerald isn’t a penetrator by nature, offering limited pass-rush juice, but his ability to clog lanes shows up on early downs. He earned All-Conference honorable mention for his consistency. NFL teams will view him as a rotational nose or one-tech who can stabilize the interior and contribute in short-yardage situations.

name: Barion Brown
summary: Barion Brown (6’1/182) enters the 2026 draft as the most dangerous "open-field" weapon in the class after a dynamic 2025 campaign at LSU. Combining 10.49-second 100-meter speed with elite vision, Brown functions as a triple-threat nightmare who demands a "defensive spy" on every snap. While his lean frame raises durability concerns and his route-running remains a developmental project, his record-breaking kick-return utility and "Tyreek Hill-esque" acceleration offer a legitimate WR1 ceiling. Following a Senior Bowl week where he clocked the fastest practice speed (22.4 MPH), Brown has vaulted into late-first-round consideration. He is a "home-run" specialist with the rare twitch to turn any routine screen or return into a game-changing score.

name: Caleb Douglas
summary: Caleb Douglas (6’4/205) operates as a "towering" vertical weapon who utilized a 2025 campaign at Texas Tech to record 846 yards and 7 touchdowns. This former Florida transfer combines elite length with a "smooth" release package, showcasing the body control to dominate 50/50 balls and high-point catches in the red zone. While scouts note he lacks elite top-end burners to consistently separate vertically, his 15.7 yards-per-catch average and "big slot" utility highlight a legitimate WR2 ceiling. Following a refined 2025 season where he sinked his hips effectively on intermediate routes, Douglas has solidified a Round 3 grade. He possesses the rare catch radius and physical "alpha" traits needed to become a primary professional goal-line weapon.

name: Ethan Burke
summary: Ethan Burke (6’5/250 est.) rotated on Texas’ edge in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pressures with quick first step and violent hand usage. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in pass-rush and run-defense snaps. Developmental edge with starter traits if strength and bend improve. Bend and corner speed remain average vs. quicker tackles; pass-rush plan is power-heavy with limited counters; leverage issues occur when pad level rises. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational disruptor in schemes that value motor and hand usage.

name: Cameron Ball
summary: Cameron Ball (6’3/290 est.) rotated in Arkansas’ interior snaps in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure in run and pass phases. Quickness for size, active hands to disengage, and power to anchor against doubles stood out when pad level stayed low. Developmental 3-technique/nose tackle with multi-alignment value and high motor in short bursts. Leverage issues arise when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters are limited with little bend against quicker guards; stamina drops in extended reps. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational depth if technique is refined and strength is added.

name: Trey Moore
summary: Trey Moore (6’3”, 255 lbs) stands out as a productive edge rusher, recording 8+ sacks and double-digit tackles for loss while consistently pressuring quarterbacks. He shows good first-step quickness, active hands, and the ability to convert speed to power. Moore flashed during Senior Bowl practices, winning one-on-one reps and elevating his draft stock. While he can be neutralized by strong tackles when his timing is off, his motor runs hot. He earned First-Team All-Conference honors and projects as a Day 2 or early Day 3 edge with rotational upside and pass-rush value.

name: Jalon Daniels
summary: Jalon Daniels (6’0/218) represents an "unmatched" athletic specimen who capped a historic six-year Kansas tenure with over 2,400 passing yards in 2025. This elite dual-threat commander combines Lamar Jackson-esque escapability with a rapid-fire release, proving his effectiveness as both a "gunslinger" and a designed-run weapon. While his significant back and shoulder medical history limits his ceiling to a "gadget" backup role, his 4.38-speed and improved "post-pressure" poise impressed scouts during the recent showcase circuit. Currently trending toward a late-round selection, Daniels offers the unique high-ceiling versatility to revolutionize a creative NFL offensive system.

name: Deven Eastern
summary: A massive presence on the interior, the 6-foot-6, 320-pound redshirt senior enters the 2026 NFL Draft as a physically imposing developmental prospect. Eastern boasts exceptional natural length and a frame capable of anchoring against the run, as evidenced by his 38 tackles and five tackles for loss during his final season with the Golden Gophers. While his raw power and pursuit speed allow him to collapse pockets, scouts note a need for improved hand-fighting consistency and a more refined pass-rush repertoire beyond his initial bull rush. After an impressive 2026 Senior Bowl showing that secured his NFL Combine invite, Eastern projects as a high-upside 3-4 defensive end or a versatile "heavy" three-technique, likely coming off the board in the middle-to-late rounds.

name: Diego Pavia
summary: Diego Pavia (5’11”, 205 lbs) brings toughness, competitiveness, and playmaking instincts under center, accounting for 3,000+ total yards and 30+ touchdowns as both a passer and runner. He thrives off-script, extending plays and punishing defenses with his legs, while showing improving anticipation as a thrower. Arm strength and size limit his ceiling, but his leadership and edge stand out. Pavia projects as a Round 6–7 pick or priority free agent, offering early value as a QB3 and scout-team weapon, with upside as a high-end backup in a movement-based offense.

name: Patrick Payton
summary: Patrick Payton (6’5”, 250 lbs) flashes high-end edge traits, combining length, burst, and power while producing double-digit sacks and tackles for loss. He wins with speed-to-power conversion and active hands, consistently stressing tackles off the edge. Payton showed well in Senior Bowl practices, confirming his athletic upside. Run defense remains a growth area, but the pass-rush juice is real. He projects as a Round 2–3 selection, offering immediate value as a rotational pass rusher, with starter upside as his run-game consistency improves.

name: Jam Miller
summary: Jamarion Miller (5’10”, 215 lbs) delivers compact power and burst from the backfield, producing 700+ rushing yards, strong yards-per-carry efficiency, and red-zone scores. He runs with good pad level, contact balance, and decisiveness, consistently finishing forward. Miller isn’t a dynamic receiver and won’t create many explosive plays, but he’s dependable. He projects as a Round 6–7 pick or priority free agent, offering early value as a short-yardage and rotational runner, with special teams upside and spot-duty potential.

name: Jeff Caldwell
summary: Jeff Caldwell (6’2/195 est.) contributed as a rotational wide receiver for Cincinnati in 2025, showing reliable hands, intermediate separation, and yards after catch on slants/screens. Route nuance and body control appeared in limited production. Developmental slot/outside receiver with dependable traits. Size aids contested-catch situations; production remains modest; press coverage at the line creates inconsistency. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth piece or special teams contributor if releases are refined and strength is added.

name: Caullin Lacy
summary: Caullin Lacy is a 5'10", 190-pound dynamic playmaker from Louisville who has solidified his status as one of the premier "all-purpose" weapons in the 2026 class. After a massive 1,316-yard season at South Alabama in 2023, Lacy transferred to the ACC where he earned Third-Team All-ACC honors despite missing early time with a broken collarbone. Scouts are enamored with his "speed for days" and elite elusiveness, evidenced by the 482 yards he gained after the catch in 2025. A true special teams ace, he was a FWAA Second-Team All-American as a returner, highlighted by two punt return touchdowns and a 100-yard kickoff return score. While his smaller frame may limit him to slot duties, his "high football IQ" and ability to win in space make him a dangerous sub-package weapon. Currently a Round 4 developmental prospect, Lacy is a high-upside target for teams needing immediate special teams impact and a shifty YAC threat.

name: Riley Nowakowski
summary: Riley Nowakowski (6’5/245 est.) contributed as a rotational tight end for Indiana in 2025, showing receptions in seam and red-zone roles with reliable hands and blocking effort on short-yardage plays. Route nuance and leaping at the catch point appeared in limited production. Developmental in-line/move tight end with receiving traits and every-down value. Blocking technique and anchor remain inconsistent against power edges; height advantage is limited in contested situations; vertical speed to stretch seams is modest. Day 3 projection (Round 4-6) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational TE2/TE3 if blocking hand placement is refined and strength is added.

name: Noah Whittington
summary: Noah Whittington (5’10/205) stands out as a "high-octane" home-run threat who spearheaded Oregon’s vertical ground game with a career-best 8.20 yards per carry. This sixth-year senior combines elite 4.38-speed with the vision to exploit perimeter creases, turning routine off-tackle concepts into explosive chunk plays. While a 2025 injury slowed his early-season momentum, his "rhythm-based" dominance during the Ducks’ late-season winning streak has vaulted his stock into the mid-round conversation. Whittington offers the "game-breaking" lateral quickness and return-game utility required for a primary rotational role. He is a "lightning-fast" playmaker for 2026.

name: Vinny Anthony II
summary: Vinny Anthony II is an ascending wide receiver prospect from Wisconsin whose draft stock significantly improved after a standout week at the 2026 Senior Bowl. Standing 6'0" and 183 pounds, Anthony impressed scouts in Mobile with a sophisticated release package and the play-strength to haul in catches away from his frame in tight coverage. While his 2025 production was modest—tallying 31 receptions for 391 yards—his versatility is a primary draw; he is a dangerous return specialist with a 95-yard kickoff return touchdown against Alabama to his credit. Scouts view him as a refined route-runner with elite hands who can contribute immediately on special teams. Currently projected as a late-round pick (6th–7th round), Anthony is one of only two Badgers invited to the 2026 NFL Combine, where he looks to solidify his place on Day 3.

name: Xavier Nwankpa
summary: Xavier Nwankpa (6’1/205 est.) contributed in Iowa’s secondary in 2025, showing range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run support and coverage. Box/single-high traits and pursuit motor appeared in rotational snaps. Developmental safety with depth upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; physicality in run support needs refinement; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth safety or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are polished.

name: Harrison Wallace III
summary: Harrison Wallace III (6’1/190 est.) contributed as a rotational wide receiver for Mississippi in 2025, showing quick separation, reliable hands, and yards after catch on intermediate routes. Route nuance and body control appeared in limited production. Developmental slot/outside receiver with dependable traits. Size aids contested-catch situations; production remains modest; press coverage at the line creates inconsistency. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth piece or special teams contributor if releases are refined and strength is added.

name: Rahsul Faison
summary: Rahsul Faison (5’11/217) represents a "violent" between-the-tackles hammer who spearheaded South Carolina’s ground game with 588 scrimmage yards in his 2025 breakout. This former Utah State star combines elite contact balance with a "piston-like" leg drive, earning a reputation for punishing defenders in the open field. While his "breakout age" (26) and banged-up shoulder have sparked draft-board debates, his elite 4.46-speed projection and "Sony Michel-style" wiggle make him a high-end rotational asset. After an active Senior Bowl showcasing his utility as a pass-catcher, Faison projects as a Day 3 value. He is the quintessential "finisher" for any physical NFL offense seeking a three-down bruiser with the functional mass to handle a professional workload.

name: Nate Boerkircher
summary: Nate Boerkircher (6'4", 250) tight end who concluded his collegiate football career as a graduate transfer with the Texas A&M Aggies after spending five seasons with the Nebraska Cornhuskers. A former walk-on from Aurora, Nebraska, he earned a scholarship in Lincoln through his reputation as a high-IQ blocker and reliable "safety valve" receiver before transferring to College Station for the 2025 season. During his lone year with the Aggies, Boerkircher emerged as a key red-zone threat, notably catching a game-winning touchdown against Notre Dame and earning a nomination for the Burlsworth Trophy, awarded to the nation’s top player who began their career as a walk-on. As of February 2026, he is officially moving toward the professional ranks, having recently showcased his versatility and "in-line" blocking skills for NFL scouts at the 2026 Senior Bowl.

name: Gary Smith III
summary: Gary Smith III (6’2/340) provides a "monstrous" interior presence who anchored UCLA’s 2025 defensive line with 39 tackles and a reputation as an unmovable run-stuffing anchor. This former Duke transfer utilizes elite "knock-back" strength and a massive frame to neutralize double-teams, recording 1.5 TFLs while consistently resetting the line of scrimmage. While his pass-rush utility is limited to a "bull-rush" specialist, his ability to "clog the drain" against physical Big Ten rushing attacks has made him a favorite for 3-4 defensive coordinators. Following an active Senior Bowl where he showcased improved "post-pressure" stamina, Smith’s stock has solidified in the late-round range. He is a high-value "short-yardage" specialist with the functional mass to start immediately.

name: Chase Roberts
summary: Chase Roberts (6’2/195 est.) contributed as a rotational wide receiver for TCU in 2025, showing reliable hands, intermediate separation, and yards after catch on slants/screens. Route nuance and body control appeared in limited production. Developmental slot/outside receiver with dependable traits. Size aids contested-catch situations; production remains modest; press coverage at the line creates inconsistency. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth piece or special teams contributor if releases are refined and strength is added.

name: Luke Altmyer
summary: Luke Altmyer (6’2/205) salvaged his draft stock with a "masterclass" 2025 campaign at Illinois, leading the Illini to nine wins while recording 22 touchdowns to just five picks. This high-IQ signal-caller operates with elite "post-pressure" poise, utilizing a rapid-fire release and refined field-scanning ability to dissect complex Big Ten coverages. While he lacks the "elite" arm-strength of a top-ten pick, his 153.75 passer rating and "quarterback-friendly" decision-making have made him a favorite for West Coast offensive coordinators. Following a Senior Bowl performance where he was lauded for his "touch" on deep passes, Altmyer is firmly in the Day 2 conversation. He possesses the veteran "cool" and processing speed required to lead a professional huddle.

name: Charles Demmings
summary: Charles Demmings (6’3”, 200 lbs) brings length, instincts, and ball production to the cornerback position, recording multiple interceptions and double-digit pass breakups. He’s most comfortable in off-man and zone looks, using anticipation and closing speed to disrupt throws. Demmings can be challenged by sudden route runners and needs added strength versus physical receivers. While not a Senior Bowl standout, his production carries weight. He projects as a Round 5–6 selection, offering early value as outside corner depth and special teams, with developmental starter potential in zone-heavy schemes.

name: Xavian Sorey Jr.
summary: A 6-foot-3, 225-pound redshirt senior, Xavian Sorey Jr. has evolved from a rotational player at Georgia into a defensive cornerstone for the Razorbacks. Possessing elite sideline-to-sideline range and the athletic profile of a modern sub-package linebacker, Sorey excels in space and coverage, using his length to disrupt passing lanes. While scouts initially questioned his play strength, his 2024 breakout—leading Arkansas with 99 tackles—and a strong 2026 Senior Bowl showing have proven his ability to play downhill and finish violently. His instincts have sharpened significantly, and his background as a high school athlete translates to smooth body control and "thumper" potential against the run. Currently, Sorey projects as a mid-round pick for the 2026 NFL Draft with starter upside.

name: Eric Gentry
summary: Eric Gentry (6’6”, 235 lbs) stands out immediately due to his rare length, range, and coverage ability at linebacker, finishing with 70+ tackles, tackles for loss, and pass breakups. He covers ground effortlessly, disrupts throwing lanes, and looks natural dropping into zone coverage. Gentry can struggle taking on blocks and playing through congestion, but his athletic profile is unique. He earned All-Conference recognition and flashed in postseason evaluations. He projects as a Round 3–4 pick, contributing early in sub-packages, special teams, and coverage roles, with clear starter upside as a modern NFL linebacker.

name: Jakobe Thomas
summary: Jakobe Thomas (6’2”, 205 lbs) plays with physicality and instincts from the safety spot, finishing the season with 70+ tackles, multiple pass breakups, and interceptions. He triggers downhill decisively and isn’t shy about contact, showing value in run support and underneath zones. Thomas can be stressed by pure speed in deep coverage, but his angles and awareness limit explosive mistakes. He earned All-Conference recognition and steady draft interest. He projects as a Round 4–5 selection, with early contribution on special teams and sub-packages, and starter upside in a split-safety scheme.

name: Adam Randall
summary: Adam Randall (6’2/235) reinvented his professional outlook in 2025, successfully converting from wide receiver to a hybrid "power" running back for Clemson. This physically imposing weapon recorded 814 rushing yards and 10 touchdowns while adding 36 receptions out of the backfield, showcasing the "Cordarrelle Patterson-style" versatility that thrives in modern creative offenses. While he is still refining his vision between the tackles, his elite contact balance and "above-the-rim" catch radius create significant mismatch problems for traditional NFL linebackers. Currently projected as a mid-round value, Randall offers the rare strength-speed profile required to impact an offense as a primary goal-line hammer or a vertical seam-stretcher. He is the class’s premier offensive "chess piece" for 2026.

name: Collin Wright
summary: Collin Wright (6’1/190 est.) started at corner for Stanford in 2025, recording 33 tackles, 5 passes defended, and 1 interception while showing physicality, ball skills, and versatility in outside/nickel roles. Quick feet, competitive press technique, and instincts in zone/match coverage appeared. Developmental corner with boundary/slot upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent; technique lapses occur during transitions. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth corner or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are refined.

name: Brent Austin
summary: Brent Austin (6’1”, 225 lbs) plays the linebacker position with urgency and physicality, racking up 90+ tackles, multiple tackles for loss, and forced turnovers. He diagnoses quickly, fills gaps decisively, and shows leadership directing traffic pre-snap. Coverage range is serviceable but not a strength, particularly against athletic tight ends. Austin earned All-Conference honors for his production and consistency. While he wasn’t a Senior Bowl invitee, his tape reflects reliability. He projects as a Day 3 linebacker with early special teams impact and rotational value in run-heavy defensive packages.

name: Nick Barrett
summary: Nick Barrett (6’5”, 265 lbs) operates as a high-effort edge defender, finishing with 5+ sacks and consistent pressure production. He wins with motor, length, and physicality rather than burst, setting a firm edge against the run. Barrett lacks elite bend and closing speed, which caps his pass-rush ceiling, but his reliability stands out. Without Senior Bowl exposure, he remains more projection-based. He profiles as a Round 6–7 pick, contributing early as a rotational edge and special teams player, with staying power as depth along the defensive front.

name: Red Murdock
summary: Red Murdock (6’2/230 est.) started at linebacker for Buffalo in 2025, contributing solid range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run defense and coverage. Sideline-to-sideline traits and pursuit motor appeared in rotational snaps. Developmental off-ball linebacker with three-down potential. Physicality and shedding remain inconsistent against climbing blockers; slow reads and false steps occur in aggressive run fits; added strength and mass are needed to anchor in the box. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth linebacker and special teams contributor if run-game technique improves.

name: Logan Fano
summary: Logan Fano (6’4/245) functions as a relentless edge-rusher who successfully navigated a third career ACL recovery to anchor Utah’s 2025 defensive line. This "non-stop" disruptor utilizes a sophisticated array of hand-counters and a devastating speed-to-power transition to consistently collapse pocket integrity. While his medical history remains a primary "minefield" for NFL evaluators, his high-IQ diagnostic skills and 85.0 PFF run-defense grade suggest a pro-ready floor. Currently projected as a Day 3 sleeper, Fano possesses the rare mental toughness and "slippery" pass-rush traits to develop into a consistent situational contributor in any NFL front.

name: DeShon Singleton
summary: DeShon Singleton (6’0”, 230 lbs) anchors the defense as a physical, downhill linebacker who filled the stat sheet with 100+ tackles, double-digit tackles for loss, and multiple sacks. He diagnoses quickly, triggers downhill with force, and shows leadership as the communicator in the front seven. Singleton isn’t a rangy coverage linebacker and can be stressed by speed in space, but his instincts and tackling reliability stand out. He earned First-Team All-Conference recognition and flashed well in postseason all-star practices. Projects as a Day 3 linebacker with special teams value and early-down defensive utility.

name: Jack Pyburn
summary: Jack Pyburn (6’4/264) rotated on LSU’s edge in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pressures with length and power to convert speed to bull rush. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in pass-rush and run-defense snaps. Developmental edge with starter traits if bend and technique improve. Corner speed and bend remain average vs. quicker tackles; pass-rush counters are limited; leverage issues occur when pad level rises against stronger blockers. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational disruptor in schemes that value length and power.

name: Xavier Scott
summary: Xavier Scott (5’10”, 200 lbs) brings explosiveness and versatility as a running back, totaling 900+ all-purpose yards with contributions as a rusher and receiver. He shows excellent lateral agility, burst through creases, and value on third downs. Scott creates mismatches in space but lacks ideal size for sustained interior work and can be inconsistent in pass protection. While not a postseason award winner, his production and versatility stand out. He profiles as a change-of-pace NFL back with receiving upside, best suited for a committee role that maximizes his space and matchup ability.

name: Kaleb Proctor
summary: Kaleb Proctor is the premier small-school sleeper of the 2026 NFL Draft, a high-motor interior defensive lineman who dominated the FCS ranks. Standing 6'3" and 280 pounds, the reigning Southland Conference Player of the Year led the FCS with 9.0 sacks in 2025, parlaying his elite first-step quickness and violent hand usage into a 2026 NFL Scouting Combine invitation. Proctor excels as a penetrating three-technique, using a well-versed pass-rush repertoire—including a signature double-hand swipe—to disrupt pockets. While scouts admire his 4.83 speed and relentless pursuit, he is considered undersized for a traditional NFL interior role and can struggle to anchor against double teams. Currently projected as a Day 3 riser, he projects as a high-upside rotational pass rusher who could thrive in a movement-based defensive front.

name: Hank Beatty
summary: Hank Beatty (5’11/185) represents a "Swiss Army Knife" weapon who made history in 2025 as the only player with passing, rushing, receiving, and return touchdowns. This high-IQ technician excels in the slot, utilizing "pro-ready" route savvy and elite short-area burst to turn shallow crossers into chunk gains. While his diminutive frame limits his boundary utility, his 1,123 all-purpose yards and record-breaking punt-return ability solidify a Day 3 grade. After a standout Senior Bowl where he proved his reliability as a "chain-mover," Beatty offers the scheme-diverse utility needed for any creative NFL offensive system in 2026.

name: Cole Wisniewski
summary: Cole Wisniewski (6’1/200 est.) contributed in Texas Tech’s secondary in 2025, showing range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run support and coverage. Box/single-high traits and pursuit motor appeared in rotational snaps. Developmental safety with depth upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; physicality in run support needs refinement; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth safety or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are polished.

name: Desmond Reid
summary: Desmond Reid is an electrifying, diminutive playmaker whose versatility as a "human highlight reel" has defined his draft profile. Standing 5’8” and weighing 175 pounds, the Pittsburgh star is an elite dual-threat weapon, finishing the 2025 season with over 1,500 all-purpose yards and 14 touchdowns. Scouts rave about his world-class acceleration and "stop-on-a-dime" agility, which allows him to thrive as a receiver out of the slot and a dynamic punt returner. While his small frame raises durability concerns and limits his pass-protection upside, his explosive playmaking ability draws comparisons to Darren Sproles. Currently projected as a Day 2 or early Day 3 pick, Reid is viewed as a high-upside "gadget" weapon who can immediately transform an NFL team's special teams and sub-package offense.

name: Kaelon Black
summary: Kaelon Black (5’10/195 est.) contributed as a rotational running back in 2025, showing quick burst, vision in finding cutback lanes, and yards after contact in limited touches. Patience to let blocks develop and elusiveness in space appeared. Developmental change-of-pace back with committee upside. Limited volume in complementary role; ball security concerns under contact; pass-protection technique needs refinement against blitzers; lacks power to handle loaded boxes consistently. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth RB or special teams contributor if vision and elusiveness translate.

name: Terion Stewart
summary: Terion Stewart (5’9”, 205 lbs) brings explosive juice to the backfield, totaling 900+ rushing yards, strong yards-per-carry efficiency, and receiving production. He accelerates quickly through creases and creates chunk plays with lateral agility and contact balance. Stewart’s size limits durability concerns and pass-protection reps, but his playmaking is evident. He projects as a Round 5–6 selection, with immediate impact as a change-of-pace and third-down back, capable of producing early in a committee that emphasizes space and tempo.

name: TJ Guy
summary: TJ Guy (6’4/250 est.) rotated on Michigan’s edge in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pressures with quick first step and violent hand usage. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in pass-rush and run-defense snaps. Developmental edge with starter traits if strength and bend improve. Bend and corner speed remain average vs. quicker tackles; pass-rush plan is power-heavy with limited counters; leverage issues occur when pad level rises. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational disruptor in schemes that value motor and hand usage.

name: Owen Heinecke
summary: Owen Heinecke is a 6-foot-1, 227-pound linebacker whose unconventional path—transferring to Oklahoma as a preferred walk-on after playing college lacrosse at Ohio State—concluded with a breakout 2025 season. An All-SEC Second Team selection, Heinecke is a "point-and-shoot" linebacker characterized by elite straight-line speed (reaching over 20 MPH) and a violent playing style. He led Oklahoma in several key metrics in 2025, recording 74 tackles, 12 tackles for loss, and 3 sacks. While scouts praise his downhill burst and special teams value, he faces criticisms regarding his short arms, which contribute to a higher missed tackle rate (20.5%), and a lack of lateral agility. Currently a 2026 NFL Combine invitee, he projects as a high-motor depth piece or special teams ace.

name: Josh Moten
summary: Josh Moten (6’2”, 215 lbs) offers a balanced linebacker profile, producing 90+ tackles, tackles for loss, and coverage impact over the season. He reads quickly, flows laterally with control, and shows comfort dropping into hook and flat zones. Moten isn’t a thumper and can struggle taking on blocks inside, but his range and awareness fit today’s game. He projects as a Round 6–7 pick, contributing early on special teams and sub-packages, with upside as a rotational linebacker in space-oriented defenses.

name: Tyren Montgomery
