summary: Tyren Montgomery (often referred to as Tyron Montgomery) is the "wildcard" of the 2026 class, a 6'1", 190-pound wide receiver from DIII John Carroll whose path to the draft is entirely unconventional. A former LSU walk-on basketball player who didn't play high school football, Montgomery transitioned to the gridiron late but exploded for a record-breaking 1,528 receiving yards and 119 receptions in 2025. Scouts are enamored with his "basketball-to-grass" athleticism, specifically his elite body control in the air and polished route running that betrayed his lack of experience during a standout Senior Bowl week. While his DIII competition level is a question mark, his ability to create separation against top-tier corners has scouts buzzing. Likely a Day 3 pick or priority free agent, his massive ceiling makes him a high-upside developmental project.

name: Devonta Smith
summary: Devonta Smith (5’11/180 est.) contributed in Notre Dame’s secondary rotation in 2025, showing quick feet, competitive ball skills, and solid run support in nickel and boundary snaps. Press technique at the line, mirroring underneath, and instincts in zone/match coverage showed promise. Developmental corner with nickel/slot upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent; technique lapses occur during transitions. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth corner or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are refined.

name: Devon Marshall
summary: Devon Marshall (5’11”, 190 lbs) operates as a versatile defensive back, contributing tackles, pass breakups, and turnover production from nickel and safety alignments. He shows good instincts, competitive toughness, and willingness in run support. Marshall can be challenged by size mismatches downfield, but his awareness helps mitigate coverage lapses. He projects as a Round 6 pick, with immediate contribution on special teams and sub-packages, and the potential to carve out a role as a matchup defender inside.

name: Robert Spears-Jennings
summary: Robert Spears-Jennings (6’1/217) operates as an "instinctive" hard-hitter who anchored the Oklahoma secondary with a breakout 63-tackle campaign in 2024 before declaring as a junior. This former four-star recruit combines elite 4.45-speed with the physical play-strength to record 2.5 sacks and force four fumbles, proving he is a true three-down "enforcer." While he occasionally struggles with over-aggressive pursuit angles, his natural ball-skills and pattern-matching agility make him a defensive coordinator’s dream for multiple secondary roles. Currently projected as a consensus top-75 pick, Spears-Jennings offers the high-end processing and "alpha" mentality required to lead a professional huddle. He is a high-floor safety prospect with the versatility to play in the box or deep-third.

name: Dezhaun Stribling
summary: De’Zhaun Stribling (6’2/210) provides an "explosive" deep-threat profile, having recorded 811 yards and 6 touchdowns for Ole Miss during a standout 2025 campaign. This Hawaii native combines long, gazelle-like strides with the body control needed to win "50-50" balls along the boundary. While he occasionally struggles with focus-drops, his 14.7 yards-per-catch average and "catch-to-attack" mentality highlight a high-end WR2 ceiling. After a productive Senior Bowl showcasing his ability to stack elite corners, Stribling has vaulted into the Day 2 conversation. He is a premier vertical weapon with the twitch to dominate pro secondaries.

name: Jalen Huskey
summary: Jalen Huskey (6’1/195) functions as a "sticky" man-coverage specialist who utilized a productive 2025 season at Maryland to showcase elite diagnostic instincts. During the 2026 Shrine Bowl practice sessions, Huskey impressed scouts with multiple pass breakups and a rare ability to "click-and-close" on short-area targets. While he needs to continue refining his tackling consistency against larger "power" receivers, his recovery speed and "ball-hawk" mentality make him a defensive coordinator’s dream for nickel or boundary roles. Currently trending as a late-round sleeper, Huskey offers the rare length and "alpha" competitive grit required to stabilize an NFL sub-package. He is a pro-ready playmaker with the diagnostic skills to become a consistent turnover generator at the next level.

name: Justin Jefferson
summary: Justin Jefferson (6’1”, 200 lbs) brings competitiveness and awareness to the secondary, finishing with 60+ tackles, pass breakups, and occasional turnover production. He plays with good eye discipline, takes solid angles, and communicates well on the back end. Jefferson isn’t a high-end athlete and can be tested vertically, but his reliability shows up snap to snap. He projects as a Round 6–7 pick, offering early contribution on special teams and as safety/nickel depth, with value in split-safety and zone-heavy systems.

name: Jalen Stroman
summary: Jalen Stroman (6’1”, 210 lbs) brings versatility to the secondary, posting 80+ tackles, tackles for loss, and pass breakups while aligning at safety and nickel. He’s physical in the box, effective blitzing, and competitive in coverage underneath. Deep range is adequate but not elite, making role clarity important. Stroman earned All-Conference honors for his production and consistency. He projects as a Round 4–5 pick, with early contribution on special teams and sub-packages, and a clear path to starting snaps in hybrid safety roles.

name: Eric O'Neill
summary: Eric O’Neill (6’3/261) successfully navigated the jump to Power-4 football in 2025, leading Rutgers with 37 tackles and a "game-wrecking" 10-tackle performance against Maryland. This former James Madison standout utilizes "violent" hand-usage and a wrestler’s understanding of leverage to compensate for his lack of "elite" vertical twitch. While evaluators note his ceiling may be that of a situational pro, his 13.0 sacks in 2024 and refined diagnostic skills against the run suggest a very high professional floor. Currently projected as a late-round value or priority free agent, O’Neill offers the "non-stop" motor and veteran technical proficiency needed to impact an NFL rotation. He is the quintessential high-effort specialist for any physical professional defensive front.

name: Robert Henry Jr.
summary: Robert Henry Jr (5’9/205) enters the 2026 draft cycle as one of the nation’s most explosive "home-run" threats, fresh off a 2025 campaign that earned him First-Team All-AAC honors and a Doak Walker Award semifinalist nod. This former JUCO superstar (NJCAA Player of the Year) became only the third player in UTSA history to crack the 1,000-yard rushing barrier, totaling 1,045 yards and 11 total touchdowns while averaging a staggering 6.9 yards per carry. Scouts rave about his "track-star" recovery burst, evidenced by six plays of 70+ yards this past season. Despite missing two games to injury, his dominant 177-yard performance against Texas A&M proved he can dismantle elite defensive fronts. Currently a Senior Bowl and East-West Shrine Bowl invitee, Henry projects as a high-upside Day 3 riser with "lightning-in-a-bottle" utility for a modern NFL committee.

name: Lance Mason
summary: Lance Mason (6’0”, 195 lbs) plays with confidence and ball awareness in the secondary, recording multiple interceptions and double-digit pass breakups. He anticipates routes well and competes at the catch point, particularly in off-man and zone looks. Mason can be challenged by bigger receivers in press situations, but his instincts help offset physical limitations. He profiles as a Round 5–6 pick, projecting early as a nickel depth corner and core special teamer, with potential to earn expanded coverage snaps in the right scheme.

name: Romello Brinson
summary: Romello Brinson (6’1”, 200 lbs) provides size and toughness at wide receiver, producing 600+ yards and multiple touchdowns while working the middle of the field. He shows strong hands, body control, and willingness to block, making him quarterback-friendly on contested throws. Separation against top corners can be inconsistent, and long speed isn’t a defining trait. He projects as a Round 6 pick, offering early value as a possession receiver and special teams contributor, with WR4 upside in structured passing games.

name: James Thompson Jr.
summary: James Thompson Jr. is a physically imposing 6-foot-5, 310-pound defensive lineman who concluded his collegiate career at Illinois after transferring from Wisconsin. A resilient veteran who overcame Achilles and pectoral injuries, Thompson Jr. earned 2025 All-Big Ten Honorable Mention honors, recording 34 tackles and 1.5 sacks while starting every game. Scouts value his alignment-versatile frame, noting his ability to play multiple techniques across the defensive front. He excels as a high-motor run defender with "brute strength" and a powerful anchor that allows him to penetrate the interior or stack blockers. While his pass-rush repertoire is considered average and lacks elite lateral explosiveness, his recent East-West Shrine Bowl performance and 2026 NFL Combine invitation solidify his status as a dependable, lunch-pail trench prospect.

name: Jimmy Rolder
summary: Jimmy Rolder is a physically imposing 6-foot-2, 240-pound linebacker who recently declared for the 2026 NFL Draft after a breakout senior campaign at Michigan. After being limited by injuries earlier in his career, Rolder ascended to a starting role in 2025, leading the Wolverines with 73 tackles, seven tackles for loss, and two sacks. Scouts highlight his high football IQ, impressive closing speed, and surprising versatility; while he projects as a traditional MIKE linebacker, he has also shown the ability to slide outside and contribute in man-to-man coverage. A Second-team All-Big Ten selection and three-time Academic All-Big Ten honoree, Rolder has bolstered his draft stock with a high-motor playing style and disruptive blitzing ability, earning him an invite to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine.

name: Kaleb Elarms-Orr
summary: Kaleb Elarms-Orr (6’1”, 225 lbs) flashes modern linebacker traits, combining range and instincts while recording 80+ tackles, pass breakups, and backfield disruption. He moves well in space, shows comfort in zone coverage, and closes quickly when triggering downhill. Elarms-Orr can be overwhelmed by blocks inside and needs added functional strength, but his athletic profile fits today’s game. He projects as a Round 4–5 pick, contributing early in sub-packages and special teams, with upside as a three-down linebacker in space-oriented schemes.

name: Chip Trayanum
summary: Chip Trayanum (5’11/205 est.) contributed as a rotational running back for Toledo in 2025, showing quick burst, vision in finding cutback lanes, and yards after contact in limited touches. Patience to let blocks develop and elusiveness in space appeared. Developmental change-of-pace back with committee upside. Limited volume in complementary role; ball security concerns under contact; pass-protection technique needs refinement against blitzers; lacks power to handle loaded boxes consistently. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth RB or special teams contributor if vision and elusiveness translate.

name: Miller Moss
summary: Miller Moss (6’2/205) salvaged his draft stock during a gutsy 2025 campaign at Louisville, recording 22 touchdowns and showcasing excellent command of a pro-style system. This "gutsy" commander operates with a high-IQ approach, utilizing rapid-fire processing and elite "clean-pocket" accuracy to distribute the ball to his weapons. While scouts note his average arm talent and "emergency QB3" physical ceiling, his improved poise under heavy pressure and "quarterback-friendly" decision-making impressed evaluators during the recent showcase circuit. Projected as a Day 3 value, Moss offers the reliable leadership and veteran "cool" needed for a backup role.

name: Brandon Cleveland
summary: Brandon Cleveland (6’4/315) functions as a "violent" interior presence who anchored NC State’s 2025 front with 36 tackles and 7.0 TFLs. This former baseball standout utilizes "heavy hands" and elite upper-body strength to displace interior linemen and erase running lanes. While he isn't a "finesse" pass-rusher, his ability to "forklift" centers off their spots creates consistent chaos in the pocket. Following an active Senior Bowl week where he consistently held the point against double-teams, Cleveland’s stock has solidified in the fourth-round range. He is a high-floor, run-stuffing specialist with the mass to start immediately.

name: Mason Reiger
summary: Mason Reiger is a rising EDGE prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft who significantly boosted his stock after a dominant performance at the East-West Shrine Bowl, where he was named Defensive MVP. A 6-foot-5, 250-pound senior transfer from Louisville, Reiger anchored the Wisconsin defense in 2025 with five sacks, six tackles for loss, and 45 total pressures, ranking him among the top 25 edge rushers nationally. Scouts laud his high-intensity motor, "wicked fast" hands, and tactical rush angles, though some note he needs to add mass to consistently set the edge against pro-level tackles. Currently projected as a Day 3 pick or high-priority developmental starter, Reiger has officially been invited to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine.

name: Jack Kelly
summary: Jack Kelly (6’2/230 est.) rotated at linebacker for BYU in 2025, contributing solid range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run defense and coverage. Sideline-to-sideline traits and pursuit motor appeared in limited snaps. Developmental off-ball linebacker with three-down potential. Physicality and shedding remain inconsistent against climbing blockers; slow reads and false steps occur in aggressive run fits; added strength and mass are needed to anchor in the box. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth linebacker and special teams contributor if run-game technique improves.

name: Colbie Young
summary: Colbie Young (6’5/215) enters the 2026 cycle as the premier "mismatch" weapon at wide receiver, having utilized his 2025 campaign at Georgia to record 358 yards and a career-long 45-yard reception. This former Miami transfer utilizes elite "basketball-style" body control to dominate 50/50 balls, making him a primary target in high-leverage red-zone scenarios. While evaluators note he lacks the "elite" vertical burners of a top-ten pick, his 19 first-down receptions and ability to "box out" defenders in contested situations highlight a high-end WR2 ceiling. Currently projected as a mid-round value, Young offers the rare catch radius and physical "alpha" traits required to become a primary professional goal-line weapon. He is a scheme-proof perimeter threat.

name: Mark Gronowski
summary: Mark Gronowski (6’3/215 est.) served as Iowa’s quarterback in 2025, completing passes with good accuracy, quick release, and mobility to extend plays in limited action. Dual-threat traits and timing-route throws appeared in spot duty. Developmental quarterback with backup potential. Decision-making under pressure and pre-snap processing remain inconsistent; happy feet and hold-the-ball tendencies surface against blitzes; arm talent is solid but not elite. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as developmental backup if mechanics and reads are refined.

name: Wesley Williams
summary: Wesley Williams (6’3”, 260 lbs) shows up on tape as an effort-driven edge defender with flashes of disruption, finishing the season with moderate sack production and steady pressure totals. He plays with good motor and functional power, occasionally winning with first-step quickness and leverage. Williams wasn’t a major postseason invite, and his lack of consistent production shows up against top competition. Refinement is needed with hand usage and rush counters. NFL teams will view him as a late-round or priority free agent prospect whose value lies in developmental upside and rotational depth along the defensive front.

name: Desmond Purnell
summary: Desmond Purnell is a productive 6-foot, 232-pound linebacker for Kansas State who has emerged as a high-motor leader ahead of the 2026 NFL Draft. A redshirt senior from Topeka, Kansas, Purnell capped a standout 2025 campaign with 72 total tackles, 3.5 sacks, and two interceptions, earning All-Big 12 Honorable Mention honors. Scouts value his versatility as a former high school safety, noting his elite closing speed and ball-hawking instincts—highlighted by a momentum-swinging pick-six against TCU. While he possesses an ideal frame for a modern off-ball linebacker, draft evaluators are looking for more consistency at the point of attack against larger offensive linemen. Currently projected as a mid-to-late round prospect, Purnell’s special teams value and reliable tackling make him a strong candidate for a roster spot.

name: Miles Kitselman
summary: Miles Kitselman (6’4/245 est.) contributed as a rotational tight end for Tennessee in 2025, showing receptions in seam and red-zone roles with reliable hands and blocking effort on short-yardage plays. Route nuance and leaping at the catch point appeared in limited production. Developmental in-line/move tight end with receiving traits and every-down value. Blocking technique and anchor remain inconsistent against power edges; height advantage is limited in contested situations; vertical speed to stretch seams is modest. Day 3 projection (Round 4-6) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational TE2/TE3 if blocking hand placement is refined and strength is added.

name: Seydou Traore
summary: Seydou Traore (6’4”, 260 lbs) offers an intriguing blend of size and athleticism at tight end, producing 30+ receptions, 400+ yards, and red-zone touchdowns. He creates matchup issues working vertically and shows flashes of run-after-catch ability. Traore’s blocking consistency and technique remain limiting factors early in his career, particularly against power fronts. He projects as a Round 5 pick, contributing initially as a move tight end and red-zone target, with upside to expand his role as his blocking reliability improves.

name: Noah Thomas
summary: Noah Thomas (6’6/200) stands out as a "towering" boundary weapon who utilized a 2025 transfer to Georgia to showcase elite red-zone utility. This long-striding target excels at high-pointing the football, leveraging his massive catch radius to "neutralize" smaller corners in contested-catch scenarios. While he lacks the "explosive" short-area twitch of a primary separator, his 15.9 yards-per-reception average and improved route-stems suggest a high-floor possession role. Following a productive draft cycle that highlighted his reliable hands, Thomas projects as a mid-round gem. He offers the prototypical "above-the-rim" traits needed for an NFL "X" receiver role.

name: Aaron Graves
summary: Aaron Graves (6’5/295) operates as a "physical-first" disruptor who finished his Iowa career with 34 tackles, 6.5 TFLs, and a Pop Warner Award nomination for on-field excellence. This former elite wrestler utilizes "violent" hand-usage and a wrestler’s understanding of leverage to displace interior linemen and record five sacks in 2025. While he lacks the "freakish" lateral twitch of a top-ten pick, his 4.03 GPA and "non-stop" motor make him a favorite for defensive coordinators seeking a pro-ready 3-technique. Currently projected as a Day 2 value, Graves offers the scheme-proof reliability and high-character leadership needed to lead an NFL huddle. He is the class’s premier high-floor interior prospect with immediate starting traits for 2026.

name: Athan Kaliakmanis
summary: Athan Kaliakmanis (6’3/212 est.) served as Rutgers’ starting quarterback in 2025, completing passes with good accuracy, quick release, and mobility to extend plays. Solid arm strength and timing-route throws appeared in consistent production. Developmental pocket passer with backup potential. Decision-making under pressure and pre-snap processing remain inconsistent; happy feet and hold-the-ball tendencies surface against blitzes; arm talent is solid but not elite. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as developmental backup if mechanics and reads are refined.

name: Lake McRee
summary: Lake McRee (6’5/245 est.) contributed as a rotational tight end for USC in 2025, showing receptions in seam and red-zone roles with reliable hands and blocking effort on short-yardage plays. Route nuance and leaping at the catch point appeared in limited production. Developmental in-line/move tight end with receiving traits and every-down value. Blocking technique and anchor remain inconsistent against power edges; height advantage is limited in contested situations; vertical speed to stretch seams is modest. Day 3 projection (Round 4-6) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational TE2/TE3 if blocking hand placement is refined and strength is added.

name: George Gumbs Jr.
summary: George Gumbs Jr (6’4/250) emerged as a "high-octane" disruptor for Florida in 2025, recording 31 tackles, 6.5 TFLs, and 2.5 sacks in just nine games. This former wide receiver-turned-edge specialist utilizes "freakish" athletic tools and a slippery speed-to-power transition to harass quarterbacks, showcasing the bend needed to "run the hoop" against elite SEC tackles. While he remains "raw" in his diagnostic processing against the run, his 48 total pressures and "non-stop" pursuit motor highlight a legitimate Day 2 developmental ceiling. Currently trending as a "high-upside" riser, Gumbs offers the rare length and vertical twitch needed to become a primary situational pass-rusher. He is a premier "chess piece" for creative defensive coordinators in the 2026 cycle.

name: Kendal Daniels
summary: Kendal Daniels (6’2/205 est.) contributed in Oklahoma’s secondary in 2025, showing range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run support and coverage. Box/single-high traits and pursuit motor appeared in rotational snaps. Developmental safety with depth upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; physicality in run support needs refinement; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth safety or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are polished.

name: Cole Brevard
summary: Cole Brevard (6’4/305 est.) rotated in Texas’ interior snaps in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure in run and pass phases. Quickness for size, active hands to disengage, and power to anchor against doubles stood out when pad level stayed low. Developmental 3-technique/nose tackle with multi-alignment value and high motor in short bursts. Leverage issues arise when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters are limited with little bend against quicker guards; stamina drops in extended reps. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational depth if technique is refined and strength is added.

name: Joe Fagnano
summary: Joe Fagnano (6’2”, 225 lbs) brings experience and toughness to the quarterback position, operating efficiently in a timing-based offense while accounting for 2,400+ passing yards and 20+ total touchdowns. He’s comfortable working the middle of the field, shows good touch on short and intermediate throws, and protects the football. Arm strength is average and he’s not a creator outside structure, but his poise stands out. Fagnano projects as a Round 6–7 pick or priority free agent, offering early value as a QB3 and scout-team operator, with backup upside in West Coast-style systems.

name: Quintayvious Hutchins
summary: Quintayvious Hutchins is an ascending defensive end from Boston College who has utilized a high-effort playstyle to secure an invitation to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine. Standing 6-foot-3 and weighing 240 pounds, the Bessemer, Alabama native transitioned from linebacker to edge, where he flourished as a senior captain. In 2025, he recorded 35 tackles and two sacks, but his true draft stock surge came during the Senior Bowl, where he led the American defense with five tackles and a sack. Scouts praise his explosive first step, lateral bend, and effectiveness on stunts, though his smaller frame for a traditional defensive end remains a point of evaluation regarding his power against the run. Currently, he projects as a Day 3 selection with high sub-package potential.

name: Dane Key
summary: Dane Key (6’3/210) stands as a "refined" boundary target who spearheaded Nebraska’s 2025 vertical attack after transferring from Kentucky. This "muscular" possession weapon excels at shielding defenders with his frame, utilizing elite hand-eye coordination to secure difficult catches in tight windows. While he lacks the "elite" separation speed of a top-tier burner, his savvy release package and "professional" approach to pattern-matching make him a high-floor prospect. Projected as a mid-round value, Key offers the reliable "chain-moving" utility and blocking prowess required for a WR2 role. He is the quintessential "stabilizer" for a pro offense.

name: CJ Donaldson
summary: C.J. Donaldson (frequently referred to as CJ Donald in scouting circles) is a physically imposing, 6'2", 232-pound running back who transferred to Ohio State for his 2025 senior season. A former tight end, he retains a "bruiser" mentality, excelling in short-yardage and red-zone situations where he recorded 10 rushing touchdowns despite a shared backfield. While he lacks elite lateral twitch, his "speed-to-power" profile and experience as a reliable receiver make him a functional three-down prospect. Scouts value his ball security and high-effort pass protection, though his 3.8 yards per carry in 2025 suggests limited "home run" potential at the next level. Currently projected as a Day 3 value pick, Donaldson offers a high-floor, rotational "power back" profile for gap-scheme NFL offenses.

name: Tyler Onyedim
summary: Tyler Onyedim (6’3/290 est.) rotated in Texas A&M’s interior snaps in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure in run and pass phases. Quickness for size, active hands to disengage, and power to anchor against doubles stood out when pad level stayed low. Developmental 3-technique/nose tackle with multi-alignment value and high motor in short bursts. Leverage issues arise when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters are limited with little bend against quicker guards; stamina drops in extended reps. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational depth if technique is refined and strength is added.

name: Wade Woodaz
summary: Wade Woodaz is a versatile linebacker from Clemson who enters the 2026 NFL Draft cycle as a seasoned, high-IQ defender. Measuring 6-foot-4 and 235 pounds, Woodaz transitioned from safety/nickel to linebacker, a background that is evident in his lateral quickness and short-area burst. In 2025, he served as a permanent team captain and finished second on the team with 77 tackles and 7.0 tackles for loss. Scouts praise his "fast eyes" and patience when sifting through blocks, though some note a lack of elite recovery speed and knock-back power. Primarily projected as a Day 3 selection, his ability to handle multiple roles and contribute on special teams makes him a high-floor prospect for NFL rosters.

name: Keelan Marion
summary: Keelan Marion is a 6'0", 200-pound wideout and return ace whose stock is firmly on the rise following a standout 2025 season at Miami. While his transition from BYU to the ACC initially kept him in the Day 3 conversation, his performance on the national stage—specifically a 114-yard explosive outing against Ole Miss in the College Football Playoff—has scouts re-evaluating him as a potential Day 2 (Round 3) riser. Marion pairs NFL-caliber acceleration with the grit of a former 2024 All-American return specialist, offering immediate "four-core" special teams value. Though his raw touchdown production was modest (2 TDs), his 13.1 yards-per-catch and physical block-shedding make him a high-upside vertical threat. If he continues this momentum through the Combine, his "project" label will likely be replaced by that of a coveted, versatile playmaker.

name: Kaden Wetjen
summary: Kaden Wetjen is a 5'9", 196-pound return specialist from Iowa who has carved out a niche as one of the most electric "third-phase" weapons in the 2026 class. A two-time Big Ten Return Specialist of the Year and Consensus All-American, Wetjen finished 2025 with four total return touchdowns (three punts, one kickoff), surpassing Hawkeye legend Tim Dwight for the school record. Scouts laud his elite vision, contact balance, and decisive "one-cut" burst that thrives in space. While his 5'9" frame and unrefined route running limit his ceiling as a full-time wideout, his production as a gadget player and record-breaking returner makes him an immediate NFL asset. Currently projected as a Day 3 specialist (6th–7th round), Wetjen is a prime target for teams needing a spark in the return game.

name: Cian Slone
summary: Cian Slone (6’4/252) transitioned to NC State in 2025 as a graduate transfer, bringing a "non-stop" motor that previously earned him second-team All-Mountain West honors at Utah State. This versatile edge-rusher utilizes a sophisticated array of hand-counters and a devastating 4.5-sack season floor to consistently collapse pocket integrity from multiple alignments. While scouts note he lacks the elite "twitch" of a top-tier vertical burner, his 16 career tackles for loss and high-IQ diagnostic skills against the run make him a scheme-diverse asset for any NFL front. Currently projected as a Day 3 sleeper, Slone offers the veteran "cool" and technical proficiency required to stabilize a professional rotation. He is the quintessential high-effort, high-floor depth piece for a pro-style defense in 2026.

name: Landon Robinson
summary: Landon Robinson (5’11/291) anchored Navy’s interior in 2025 as a nose tackle, showing explosive quickness, low pad level, and power to disrupt gaps and generate pressures. High motor and athleticism for size stood out in run defense and pass-rush snaps. Developmental 3-technique/nose tackle with multi-alignment value. Leverage issues arise when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters are limited with little bend against quicker guards; stamina drops in extended reps. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational depth if technique is refined and strength is added.

name: Behren Morton
summary: Behren Morton is a 6'2", 215-pound "gunslinger" who solidified his draft stock by leading Texas Tech to its first-ever Big 12 Championship in 2025. Despite battling recurring injuries, he recorded a career-high 66% completion percentage, totaling 2,780 yards and 22 touchdowns. Morton is lauded for his "live arm," clean mechanics, and the ability to vary arm angles in off-schedule situations. While his accuracy and "improv" skills thrive in the short-to-intermediate game, scouts remain wary of his inconsistent pocket presence and "slingshot" delivery, which can lead to batted balls. One of 15 quarterbacks invited to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine, Morton is currently projected as a Day 3 developmental project (6th round) with the high-ceiling traits necessary to be a long-term NFL backup or "heir apparent."

name: Vincent Anthony Jr.
summary: Vincent Anthony Jr. (6’4/260) rotated on Duke’s edge in 2025, recording 38 tackles, 7.5 TFLs, 4.5 sacks, and 2 forced fumbles. Explosive first step, violent hand usage, and power bull rush stood out in pass-rush snaps. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in run defense. Developmental edge with starter traits if bend and technique improve. Bend and corner speed remain average vs quicker tackles; pass-rush counters limited; leverage issues occur when pad level rises. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft—rotational disruptor with upside in schemes that value motor and power.

name: Scooby Williams
summary: Scooby Williams (6’2/235 est.) rotated at linebacker for Texas A&M in 2025, contributing solid range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run defense and coverage. Sideline-to-sideline traits and pursuit motor appeared in limited snaps. Developmental off-ball linebacker with three-down potential. Physicality and shedding remain inconsistent against climbing blockers; slow reads and false steps occur in aggressive run fits; added strength and mass are needed to anchor in the box. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth linebacker and special teams contributor if run-game technique improves.

name: Max Tomczak
summary: Max Tomczak is a polished and gritty wide receiver from Youngstown State who has emerged as one of the premier FCS prospects for the 2026 NFL Draft. Standing 6-foot-0 and weighing 195 pounds, Tomczak is characterized by his "sure hands"—evidenced by zero drops during the 2024 season—and high-level route precision. In 2025, he dominated with 70 receptions for 1,021 yards and 8 touchdowns, showcasing his ability as a reliable chain-mover and clutch third-down target. While he lacks elite track speed or a dominant vertical leap, his "fast eyes," veteran-like processing, and dependable catch radius make him an ideal WR4 or slot specialist at the next level. A Senior Bowl Top 300 honoree, he projects as a late-round selection or high-priority free agent with strong special teams potential.

name: Isaiah Nwokobia
summary: Isaiah Nwokobia (6’1/202) anchored SMU’s secondary in 2025 as a physical, instinctive safety with strong downhill play and run support. He showed aggressive triggering on blocks, high football IQ in coverage, and solid tackling efficiency. Versatile zone slot/nickel traits with pursuit speed and ball disruption. Developmental safety with depth upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; physicality needs refinement against bigger frames; tackling consistency lapses in space. Late-round projection (Round 6/UDFA range) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as a reliable role player in sub-packages and special teams if coverage polish improves.

name: Jalen Catalon
summary: Jalen Catalon (5’10/202) functions as a "violent" high-IQ defensive general who successfully reinvented his career at UNLV following stops at Arkansas and Texas. Recording 36 tackles and multiple forced turnovers in 2025, Catalon combines "seek-and-destroy" diagnostic instincts with the mental toughness to overcome multiple shoulder injuries. While his durability remains the primary "minefield" for NFL evaluators, his "alpha" leadership and ability to navigate complex Big 12 and SEC coverages highlight a pro-style starter's floor. Currently trending as a Day 3 "low-risk" value, Catalon offers the high-end processing and "thumping" power required to lead a professional huddle. He is the heart of any defensive identity, offering immediate veteran stability to an NFL secondary.

name: Bauer Sharp
summary: Bauer Sharp (6’5/245 est.) contributed as a rotational tight end for LSU in 2025, showing receptions in seam and red-zone roles with reliable hands and blocking effort on short-yardage plays. Route nuance and leaping at the catch point appeared in limited production. Developmental in-line/move tight end with receiving traits and every-down value. Blocking technique and anchor remain inconsistent against power edges; height advantage is limited in contested situations; vertical speed to stretch seams is modest. Day 3 projection (Round 4-6) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational TE2/TE3 if blocking hand placement is refined and strength is added.

name: DQ Smith
summary: DQ Smith is a highly experienced, versatile safety from the University of South Carolina who officially declared for the 2026 NFL Draft after a productive four-year career. A former high school quarterback, Smith seamlessly transitioned to the secondary, appearing in 48 games with 45 starts and recording 228 career tackles, five interceptions, and 16 pass deflections. Standing 6-foot-1 and weighing 219 pounds, he possesses NFL-caliber size and a high football IQ, often utilized in single-high coverage, the slot, or as an overhang defender in the box. Scouts value his downhill aggressiveness and reliable tackling—leading his team with 64 tackles in 2025—though some evaluators note a high-waisted build that can occasionally affect his lateral transitions. Projected as a mid-to-late round prospect or high-priority free agent, his extensive SEC starting experience and special teams background make him a high-floor defensive back.

name: Jalen McMurray
summary: Jalen McMurray (6’1”, 200 lbs) brings consistency and physicality to the secondary, finishing with 70+ tackles and multiple pass breakups. He plays under control, takes solid angles, and communicates well on the back end. McMurray lacks elite range but compensates with discipline and reliability. Without major postseason exposure, he remains a depth projection. He profiles as a Round 6–7 selection, contributing early on special teams and as a safety/nickel reserve, with value in split-safety looks.

name: Chamon Metayer
summary: Chamon Metayer is a physically imposing tight end from Arizona State who officially declared for the 2026 NFL Draft following a decorated collegiate career. Standing 6-foot-4 and weighing 255 pounds, the former four-star recruit blossomed into a reliable dual-threat weapon, earning Second-team All-Big 12 honors in 2024. Over two seasons in Tempe, he amassed 681 receiving yards and nine touchdowns, including a 2025 campaign where he recorded 38 receptions for 375 yards. Scouts praise Metayer for his wide catch radius, sharp route running—particularly over the middle—and his "exceptionally productive" blocking that helped anchor the Sun Devils' run game. Often projected as a day-three pick, his combination of red-zone utility and high football IQ makes him an intriguing "TE-mpe" prospect for NFL schemes seeking a versatile "Y" or "F" tight end.

name: Skyler Thomas
summary: Skyler Thomas is a productive and versatile defensive back from Oregon State who has emerged as a consistent playmaker in the secondary. Measuring 6-foot-2 and 212 pounds, Thomas possess the prototypical size and frame for a modern NFL safety. A highly durable and active defender, he led the Beavers in tackles during back-to-back seasons, recording 81 tackles in 2024 and 78 tackles in 2025. Scouts highlight his impressive ball skills and coverage range, evidenced by his 13 pass breakups and two interceptions over his final two collegiate campaigns. Known for his reliable tackling and high football IQ, Thomas projects as a mid-to-late round prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, offering immediate value as a deep-safety hybrid or a core special teams contributor.

name: Jaden Dugger
summary: Jaden Dugger is an ascending defensive prospect from Louisiana whose elite physical profile makes him one of the most intriguing "hidden gems" of the 2026 NFL Draft. Standing at a towering 6-foot-5 and weighing approximately 240–250 pounds, the former Georgetown safety has successfully transitioned to linebacker, showcasing rare sideline-to-sideline range and fluidity in coverage. During a breakout 2025 season, he recorded 125 tackles, 13.0 tackles for loss, and 4.0 sacks, earning First Team All-Sun Belt honors. Scouts are particularly enamored with his 35-inch arms and explosive closing speed, which allow him to disrupt passing lanes and trigger quickly in run defense. While he is still developing his block-shedding and hand usage, Dugger’s performance at the East-West Shrine Bowl solidified his status as a high-upside Day 2 or Day 3 selection with modern nickel-backer versatility.

name: Jordan Hudson
summary: Jordan Hudson (6’0”, 185 lbs) brings burst and versatility to the receiver position, contributing explosive plays, jet motion usage, and return production. He threatens defenses horizontally, shows quick acceleration, and can uncover on short routes. Hudson lacks size and struggles through contact, limiting contested-catch situations. He projects as a Round 6–7 pick or priority free agent, contributing early as a gadget receiver and return option, with upside if he expands his route tree and play strength.

name: Jackie Marshall
summary: Jackie Marshall (6’3/290 est.) rotated in Baylor’s interior snaps in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure in run and pass phases. Quickness for size, active hands to disengage, and power to anchor against doubles stood out when pad level stayed low. Developmental 3-technique/nose tackle with multi-alignment value and high motor in short bursts. Leverage issues arise when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters are limited with little bend against quicker guards; stamina drops in extended reps. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational depth if technique is refined and strength is added.

name: Clay Patterson
summary: Clay Patterson (6’3/285) provides an "effort-first" interior presence who transitioned from Yale and UNLV to lead Stanford’s 2025 defensive line. Recording 4.0 sacks and 5.5 TFLs, Patterson utilizes a violent first-step and "slippery" hand usage to penetrate gaps. While he lacks the sheer mass to handle heavy NFL double-teams as a true nose, his versatility as a 3-technique makes him a valuable rotational asset. Following an active Senior Bowl week where he consistently collapsed the pocket, Patterson’s stock has risen into the Day 3 range as a high-value, high-motor interior depth piece.

name: Jordan van den Berg
summary: Jordan van den Berg (6’4”, 295 lbs) flashes interior disruption with multiple sacks and tackles for loss, showing quick hands and good leverage for his size. He penetrates effectively on stunts and twists, collapsing pockets when his timing is right. Van den Berg can struggle holding up against double teams and needs more consistency anchoring versus the run. He wasn’t a Senior Bowl headliner, but his athletic interior profile draws interest. With refinement, he projects as a rotational 3-tech defensive tackle capable of contributing in pass-rush packages and sub fronts.

name: Carsen Ryan
summary: Carsen Ryan (6’4/250) solidified his status as a "quarterback’s best friend" during BYU’s historic 11-win 2025 season. This former Utah and UCLA standout operates as a polished "Y-iso" target, utilizing elite body control and reliable hands to record multiple red-zone touchdowns. While he lacks the "explosive" vertical speed of a primary deep-threat, his 15.1 yards-per-catch average and high-IQ ability to exploit zone soft spots impressed scouts during the recent showcase circuit. Following a 2025 campaign where he served as a primary blocking anchor in the Big 12, Ryan has solidified a Day 3 grade. He possesses the professional frame and veteran "cool" required to become a reliable second tight end in a heavy-set NFL personnel grouping.

name: Sabastian Harsh
summary: Sebastian Harsh (6’3/263) solidified his "game-wrecker" status in 2025, recording 4 sacks and a team-high 48 pressures for NC State after transferring from the Mountain West. This "lean and speedy" defensive end utilizes a "violent" punch at the point of attack and a wrestler’s understanding of leverage to disrupt pocket integrity. While he lacks elite vertical burners, his 91.5 PFF defensive grade in the season opener proved his ability to dominate elite competition. Following a refined 2025 campaign where he missed just seven tackles, Harsh has emerged as a consensus top-150 prospect for 2026. He offers the high-end processing, motor, and "slippery" hand-usage required to become a primary rotational edge-rusher in any professional defensive front.

name: DJ Harvey
summary: DJ Harvey (5’11/190) represents the "technician’s ideal" at cornerback, having anchored the Washington secondary with a "sticky" man-coverage profile throughout 2025. This former USC and San Jose State standout excels at pattern-matching, utilizing "patient hands" and elite hip fluidity to neutralize shifty vertical threats. While his lack of "freakish" height can make him a target for larger "X" receivers in the red zone, his recovery speed and 12 career pass deflections highlight a high-end starter's floor. After a "dominant" Senior Bowl showing where he erased top-tier wideouts in one-on-ones, Harvey’s stock is firmly in the Day 2 conversation. He is a pro-ready "nickel" or boundary hybrid with the IQ to stabilize an NFL secondary immediately.

name: Keyshawn James-Newby
summary: Keyshawn James-Newby is a productive, high-motor EDGE rusher from New Mexico who has successfully climbed the collegiate ranks from NAIA Montana Tech to the FBS. Standing 6-foot-3 and weighing 245 pounds, James-Newby is considered one of the most natural pass rushers in the 2026 NFL Draft class, evidenced by a stellar 2025 season where he recorded 9.0 sacks, 15.0 tackles for loss, and a conference-leading 66 pressures. He possesses an explosive first step, elite hand-fighting skills, and a relentless "twitched-up" play style that allows him to win 37.5% of his attempts against true pass sets. While scouts praise his situational pass-rushing dominance, there are concerns regarding his run defense and frame, leading many to project him as a Day 3 selection or a developmental "situational specialist" for an NFL defense.

name: Wesley Bissainthe
summary: Wesley Bissainthe (6’2/230 est.) rotated at linebacker for Miami in 2025, contributing solid range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run defense and coverage. Sideline-to-sideline traits and pursuit motor appeared in limited snaps. Developmental off-ball linebacker with three-down potential. Physicality and shedding remain inconsistent against climbing blockers; slow reads and false steps occur in aggressive run fits; added strength and mass are needed to anchor in the box. Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth linebacker and special teams contributor if run-game technique improves.

name: Tyreek Chappell
summary: Tyreek Chappell (6’1”, 190 lbs) competes with urgency and physicality in the secondary, posting 60+ tackles, pass breakups, and alignment versatility. He’s comfortable in man coverage, shows willingness in run support, and brings good recovery speed. Chappell can lose discipline at the top of routes, leading to separation against polished receivers. He projects as a Round 6–7 pick, contributing early on special teams and as depth at corner, with upside as a matchup-based defender in press-capable schemes.

name: Jamal Haynes
summary: Jamal Haynes (5’9”, 200 lbs) brings explosive versatility to the backfield, totaling 1,000+ all-purpose yards and double-digit touchdowns as both a runner and receiver. He accelerates rapidly, shows excellent burst through creases, and consistently stresses defenses in space. Haynes is undersized for sustained interior work and can be inconsistent in pass protection, but his juice is undeniable. He earned All-Conference honors and national attention for big-play production. He projects as a Round 4–5 pick, contributing immediately as a change-of-pace back, third-down weapon, and return threat in spread-oriented offenses.

name: Zavion Thomas
summary: Zavion Thomas (5’10/192) functions as a "human joystick" who utilized his 4.32-speed to revitalize LSU’s vertical and return games in 2025. This shifty, explosive separator thrives on designed touches and underneath routes, turning routine catches into chunk gains through elite acceleration. While his diminutive frame limits his red-zone utility, his vision as a return specialist and versatility to align in the slot make him a primary gadget weapon. Following a productive Senior Bowl showing, Thomas profiles as a Day 3 value. He is the quintessential "chain-mover" for creative offensive coordinators in 2026.

name: Jalen Walthall
summary: Jalen Walthall (6’0/190 est.) contributed as a rotational wide receiver for UAB in 2025, showing quick separation, reliable hands, and yards after catch on intermediate routes. Route nuance and body control appeared in limited production. Developmental slot/outside receiver with dependable traits. Size aids contested-catch situations; production remains modest; press coverage at the line creates inconsistency. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth piece or special teams contributor if releases are refined and strength is added.

name: Will Kacmarek
summary: Will Kacmarek (6’6/258) solidified his status as a "quarterback-friendly" weapon during Ohio State’s 2025 campaign after transferring from South Dakota. Recording 15 receptions for 168 yards and two touchdowns, Kacmarek functions primarily as an elite "Y" blocker with the functional mass to stonewall Big Ten edge-rushers. While he lacks the "explosive" vertical twitch of a primary seam-stretcher, his reliable hands and "basketball-style" body control make him a primary red-zone target. Currently projected as a mid-round value, Kacmarek offers the rare competitive "grit" and technical savvy required to impact a pro running game immediately. He is the quintessential pro-ready tight end with the mass and motor to stabilize an NFL "heavy" personnel grouping.

name: VJ Payne
summary: VJ Payne (6’3”, 185 lbs) brings length and speed to the cornerback position, recording double-digit pass breakups and interceptions while matching up against top receivers. He plays confidently in press and off-man looks, using reach to contest throws, though added strength is needed to handle physical wideouts. Payne’s ball skills and athletic profile stand out. He projects as a Round 5 selection, contributing early as a special teams gunner and outside corner depth, with starter upside as his frame and technique continue to develop.

name: Namdi Obiazor
summary: Namdi Obiazor (6’1/200 est.) contributed in a secondary rotation during 2025, displaying range, tackling efficiency, and instincts in run support and coverage. Box/single-high traits and pursuit motor appeared in limited snaps. Developmental safety with depth upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; physicality in run support needs refinement; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth safety or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are polished.

name: DJ Rogers
summary: DJ Rogers (6’4/245) represents a "modern" tight end specimen who utilized his 2025 campaign at TCU to cement his status as a premier red-zone mismatch. This "big-bodied" vertical threat combines elite ball-tracking with the "basketball-style" body control required to "box out" defenders in contested situations. While his in-line blocking remains a developmental hurdle, his 14.5 yards-per-reception average and ability to stretch the "seams" make him an elite "Big Slot" candidate. Following a refined Senior Bowl where he showcased improved hand-technique and "stickiness" as a route-runner, Rogers is trending toward a mid-round selection. He offers the high-upside athletic profile and massive catch radius needed to become a primary safety-valve for an NFL quarterback.

name: Dillon Bell
summary: Dillon Bell (6’1/210 est.) transitioned to a rotational wide receiver role for Georgia in 2025 after prior snaps at tight end, showing reliable hands, intermediate separation, and yards after catch on slants/screens. Route nuance and body control appeared in limited production. Developmental slot/outside receiver with dependable traits. Size aids contested-catch situations; production remains modest; press coverage at the line creates inconsistency. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth piece or special teams contributor if releases are refined and strength is added.

name: Jaren Kanak
summary: Jaren Kanak (6’2/233) transitioned to tight end for Oklahoma in 2025, finishing with 44 receptions for 533 yards as a reliable target. Strong hands, route nuance, and yards after catch on intermediate routes appeared in his first year at the position. Developmental move tight end with receiving traits and every-down value. Blocking technique and anchor remain inconsistent against power edges; height advantage is limited in contested situations; vertical speed to stretch seams is modest. Day 3 projection (Round 4-6) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational TE2/TE3 if blocking hand placement is refined and strength is added.

name: Jacob Thomas
summary: Jacob Thomas is an exceptionally versatile and athletic safety from James Madison who has evolved from a former walk-on into a team captain and Second Team All-Sun Belt standout. Standing 6-foot-1 and weighing 212 pounds, Thomas is a high-motor defender with an aggressive downhill trigger and extensive experience playing in the box, deep safety, and the slot. During the 2025 season, he recorded 71 tackles, 2.0 sacks, and 2 interceptions, showcasing elite reactive instincts and a high coverage IQ. Scouts view him as a premier "big-nickel" prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft, praising his smooth backpedal and ability to cover tight ends in man-to-man schemes. While his zone instincts and top-end speed are areas for refinement, his relentless work ethic and special teams upside make him a projected Day 3 selection with significant developmental potential.

name: Austin Brown
summary: Austin Brown (6’4”, 290 lbs) offers interior flexibility along the defensive line, contributing tackles for loss and pocket disruption that often doesn’t show in sack totals. He plays with good leverage, active hands, and a steady motor, particularly effective on stunts and movement fronts. Brown can struggle anchoring against double teams, limiting his early-down usage. He projects as a Round 6 pick, contributing early as a rotational interior lineman, with upside in sub-packages that emphasize penetration and movement.

name: Nyjalik Kelly
summary: Nyjalik Kelly (6’4/255 est.) rotated on UCF’s edge in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pressures with quick first step and violent hand usage. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in pass-rush and run-defense snaps. Developmental edge with starter traits if strength and bend improve. Bend and corner speed remain average vs. quicker tackles; pass-rush plan is power-heavy with limited counters; leverage issues occur when pad level rises. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational disruptor in schemes that value motor and hand usage.

name: Anthony Hankerson
summary: Anthony Hankerson is a compact, high-volume running back who concluded a prolific senior season with the Oregon State Beavers in 2025. Standing 5-foot-8 and weighing 207 pounds, Hankerson possesses a low center of gravity and a "shifty" running style that makes him difficult to bring down in the open field. He became just the sixth player in program history to record multiple 1,000-yard rushing seasons, amassing 1,086 yards and 9 touchdowns in 2025. Scouts highlight his physicality, "bellcow" durability, and improved stock as a pass protector and receiver out of the backfield. While currently holding an undrafted free-agent grade for the 2026 NFL Draft, his high motor and consistent production against Power Five competition position him as a reliable developmental prospect.

name: Michael Wortham
summary: Michael Wortham (5’9/190) emerged as the "FCS Darling" of the 2026 cycle, setting a program record with over 2,400 all-purpose yards for Montana in 2025. This "shifty" slot-weapon functions as a defensive coordinator’s nightmare, capable of aligning at receiver, running back, or even wildcat quarterback. While his diminutive frame restricts him to a "rotational" NFL role, his elite change-of-direction and 11.0 yards-per-touch average highlight a "game-breaking" floor. After proving he could handle Power-4 physical competition during the Senior Bowl, Wortham has solidified a fourth-round grade. He is the quintessential "Swiss Army Knife" for creative offenses, offering immediate high-end utility as both a return specialist and an open-field playmaker.

name: Stephen Daley
summary: Stephen Daley is an elite, high-motor defensive lineman who capped a dominant 2025 senior season by helping the Indiana Hoosiers secure a national championship. Measuring 6-foot-1 and 273 pounds, Daley is a "freak athlete" who famously ran a 10.85-second 100-meter dash in high school, a rare speed for his size. In 2025, he led the Big Ten and ranked second in the FBS with 19.0 tackles for loss, while adding 5.5 sacks and two forced fumbles. Scouts prize his explosive first step and active hands, though some note his lack of ideal length. Despite a "serious" lower-body injury suffered while celebrating the Big Ten title, his historic production has solidified him as a potential Day 3 NFL Draft prospect.

name: Isaiah Smith
summary: Isaiah Smith is a disruptive 6'4", 248-pound edge rusher for the SMU Mustangs who has established himself as a high-impact defender heading into the 2026 NFL Draft. During the 2025 season, he recorded 53 total tackles and led his unit with 8.5 sacks and 18 tackles for loss, showcasing a consistent ability to penetrate the backfield. Scouts highlight his versatility and high motor, noting his capacity to handle multiple roles across the defensive line. His physical profile is characterized by a "weak-side" speed-to-power transition that allows him to win against ACC-level offensive tackles. Smith’s production has steadily increased over three seasons, evolving from a rotational contributor into a cornerstone defender known for forced fumbles and consistent quarterback pressure.

name: Keanu Tanuvasa
summary: Keanu Tanuvasa (6’4/301) functions as a high-motor defensive tackle who transitioned seamlessly to BYU for his breakout 2025 campaign. Recording multiple tackles for loss and showcasing a "slippery" first step, Tanuvasa excels at shooting gaps and disrupting pocket integrity from the 3-technique. While he lacks the massive bulk of a true nose tackle, his active hands and "non-stop" pursuit range impressed scouts during the recent showcase circuit. Projected as a mid-round value, Tanuvasa offers the scheme-diverse interior pass-rush and high-character leadership needed to stabilize a pro defensive line immediately.

name: Preston Hodge
summary: Preston Hodge (6’0/195) emerged as the premier "lockdown" corner for Colorado in 2025, anchoring a secondary that faced high-volume vertical attacks. Recording 13 pass breakups and providing a physical presence in run support, Hodge combines elite hip fluidity with a "patient hands" approach in press-man coverage. While scouts initially questioned his top-end recovery speed, his ability to execute "dead-leg" transitions and navigate complex Big 12 zone concepts has solidified his Day 2 stock. Following a polished performance where he allowed a career-low completion percentage, Hodge offers the high-end diagnostic instincts and "alpha" mentality required to stabilize an NFL secondary. He is a pro-ready boundary technician with the competitive grit needed to neutralize modern "X" receivers.

name: David Oke
summary: David Oke (6’5”, 310 lbs) brings massive interior presence, functioning as a true space-eater with 30+ tackles and several tackles for loss while commanding double teams. Oke’s value lies in anchoring against the run, controlling gaps, and allowing linebackers to flow freely. Pass-rush production is limited, and his get-off remains average, but his strength and length are undeniable. He did not participate in postseason all-star events, keeping his profile quieter. NFL teams will view him as a rotational nose or one-tech defensive tackle best suited for early downs and short-yardage packages.

name: Bryson Eason
summary: Bryson Eason (6’4/300 est.) rotated on Tennessee’s interior in 2025, contributing tackles, TFLs, and pocket pressure with quickness for size and power to hold ground against doubles when pad level stayed low. Multi-alignment value (3-technique to nose) and short-burst motor offer developmental upside. Leverage issues appear when pad level rises against climbing blockers; pass-rush counters stay limited with little bend vs. quicker guards; stamina drops in longer series. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft as rotational depth with potential for snaps if technique and strength improve.

name: Bryan Thomas Jr.
summary: Bryan Thomas Jr. is a highly productive 6'2", 249-pound edge rusher from the University of South Carolina who has significantly elevated his draft stock during the 2025 season. A 2025 Third-Team All-SEC selection and team captain, Thomas recorded career-highs with 40 tackles, 11 tackles for loss, and 7 sacks, while leading the Gamecocks with 14 quarterback hurries. Scouts praise his relentless motor and "speed-to-power" capability, evidenced by his three forced fumbles in 2025. With 46 games of experience and elite bloodlines—his father was a former NFL first-round pick—Thomas has transitioned from a rotational player into a cornerstone defender. He recently accepted an invite to the East-West Shrine Bowl, solidifying his status as a fast-rising prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft.

name: Camron Stewart
summary: Cam’Ron Stewart is a physically imposing 6'5", 250-pound edge rusher for the Temple Owls who has emerged as a premier disruptive force in the American Athletic Conference. After transferring from Rutgers, Stewart enjoyed a breakout 2025 campaign, recording 32 total tackles, 10.5 tackles for loss, and 5 sacks. He gained national attention for his elite efficiency, leading all FBS edge rushers at one point in the season with a 30.2% pass-rush win rate. Scouts are enamored with his massive frame and "speed-to-power" capability, which earned him a staggering 97.0 PFF grade in his 2025 season opener. His combination of length and explosive closing speed makes him a high-ceiling prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft.

name: West Weeks
summary: West Weeks is a veteran 6'2", 235-pound linebacker for the LSU Tigers who has solidified his role as a disciplined, reliable defender. A former Virginia transfer, Weeks utilized a redshirt in 2024 before returning for a highly productive 2025 season, where he recorded 74 total tackles, 8 tackles for loss, and 2.5 sacks in 11 games. Scouts describe him as a "rugged and traditional" linebacker who excels in run defense and gap discipline, often serving as the steadying presence alongside his more explosive younger brother, Whit. Known for his "controlled violence" and high football IQ, Weeks displays the versatility to play multiple linebacker spots and contributes significantly on special teams. His consistency and veteran leadership make him a notable prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft.

name: Jermaine Mathews Jr.
summary: Jermaine Mathews Jr. (5’11/185) started at corner for Ohio State in 2025, recording 45 tackles, 3 interceptions, 9 pass breakups, and strong coverage grades in press-man and zone. Quick feet, ball skills, and competitive traits stood out in boundary snaps. Developmental corner with outside/nickel upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; physicality in run support needs refinement; technique lapses occur during transitions. Mid-round projection (Round 3-5) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth corner if strength is added and coverage details are polished.

name: Ahmari Harvey
summary: Ahmari Harvey is a physical 6'0", 195-pound cornerback for the Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets who solidified his professional standing during a resilient 2025 season. After a productive 2024 campaign where he recorded 63 tackles, Harvey entered his redshirt senior year as a defensive anchor, finishing 2025 with 26 total tackles, a sack, and a team-high 7 passes defended. Scouts highlight his "speed-to-ball" instincts and aggressive downhill play, reflected in his 75.7 PFF coverage grade. Despite missing time due to injury, his veteran leadership and transition from safety to a lockdown corner earned him an invitation to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine. Harvey's versatility, reliable tackling, and ability to disrupt high-level ACC receivers make him a competitive prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft.

name: J. Michael Sturdivant
summary: J.Michael Sturdivant (6’3/205) operates as a physically dominant boundary target who utilizes a massive catch radius to "envelop" smaller NFL-caliber corners. This lean, long-striding athlete thrives in the red zone, leveraging his track-and-field background to turn 50-50 balls into high-percentage receptions. While he must improve his initial burst off the line and route-tree nuances, his 20+ MPH play speed and "reliable" possession numbers solidify a late Day 2 grade. Sturdivant offers the elite high-pointing ability and "pro-ready" frame required for a primary goal-line fade target. He is a unique high-ceiling weapon for a pro offense.

name: Omega Blake
summary: O’Mega Blake is a 6’2”, 180–190 lb senior wide receiver with the Arkansas Razorbacks known for his explosive playmaking and deep-threat ability, stemming from his elite long speed and big-play production at the college level. After transferring from Charlotte—where he led the FBS in yards per catch and posted 795 yards with nine touchdowns—Blake quickly became one of Arkansas’s primary targets, finishing the 2025 season with 58 receptions for 769 yards and five scores. He excels at stretching the field and making contested grabs downfield, forcing defenses to respect his vertical threat. While still refining route precision and consistency, his combination of size, speed, and contested-catch ability gives him pro potential as a field-stressing receiver.

name: Mohamed Toure
summary: Mohamed Toure is a disruptive 6'2", 236-pound linebacker who recently completed a standout 2025 season with the Miami Hurricanes after transferring from Rutgers. Known as one of the most "violent hitters" in college football, Toure led the Hurricanes with 84 total tackles and showcased his versatility as a "pressure defender," contributing 2 sacks and 6 pass breakups. Scouts admire his muscular, NFL-ready frame and downhill explosiveness, particularly his ability to mug the A-gaps and rush from a standup edge position. Despite a career marked by significant injury setbacks, including two torn ACLs, he has displayed elite resilience and leadership as a former team captain. Toure possesses a high football IQ and a 30.2% pass-rush win rate at his peak, projecting as a potential Day 2 selection or winning starter in an attacking NFL defense.

name: Ceyair Wright
summary: Ceyair Wright (5’11/185 est.) started at corner for Nebraska in 2025, recording tackles, pass breakups, and interceptions while showing quick feet, competitive press technique, and instincts in zone/match coverage. Physicality in run support and ball skills stood out in boundary snaps. Developmental corner with outside/nickel upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent; technique lapses occur during transitions. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth corner or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are refined.

name: Eli Heidenreich
summary: Eli Heidenreich (6’0/190 est.) contributed as a rotational wide receiver for Navy in 2025, showing reliable hands, intermediate separation, and yards after catch on slants/screens. Route nuance and body control appeared in limited production. Developmental slot/outside receiver with dependable traits. Size aids contested-catch situations; production remains modest; press coverage at the line creates inconsistency. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth piece or special teams contributor if releases are refined and strength is added.

name: Kody Huisman
summary: Kody Huisman is a high-motor interior defensive lineman who successfully transitioned from a championship-winning career at North Dakota State to the Power Four level at Virginia Tech. Standing 6-foot-4 and weighing 297 pounds, his primary strengths are his elite run defense (86.6 PFF grade in 2024) and his disciplined gap integrity. He is a cerebral defender with a "coach on the field" mentality and notable special teams value, including a history of blocked kicks. His draft potential currently sits as a Day 3/Priority Free Agent prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft. Weaknesses include limited twitch as a pass rusher and the lack of a dominant, explosive first step required for high-volume sack production at the professional level.

name: Jahiem Lawson
summary: Jaheim Lawson (6’2/240) emerged as a "breakout" rotational force for Clemson in 2025, mirroring the physical "alpha" traits of his All-American brother, Shaq Lawson. This high-motor edge specialist utilizes "violent" hand-usage and a slippery speed-to-power transition to harass quarterbacks, recording 3.5 sacks in just 307 defensive snaps this past season. While scouts note he must continue adding functional mass to consistently stack-and-shed NFL-caliber tackles, his elite pursuit range and 11.0 career TFLs highlight a high-end situational floor. Currently trending as a Day 3 riser, Lawson offers the rare competitive "grit" and technical savvy required to impact a pro defensive line immediately. He is a high-upside developmental prospect with the bloodlines and motor to become a double-digit sack artist.

name: Shad Banks Jr.
summary: Shad Banks Jr. is an exceptionally athletic 6'1", 230-pound linebacker who solidified his professional outlook following a breakout 2025 season at UTSA. A former elite recruit and Junior Olympic triple jump champion, Banks transitioned from TCU to become a First-Team All-AAC performer, leading the Roadrunners with 94 tackles, 7.5 tackles for loss, and 3 interceptions. Known for his 4.4-second 40-yard dash speed, he possesses rare sideline-to-sideline range and vertical explosiveness, often utilized as a dynamic blitzer and reliable coverage asset. While scouts note he can occasionally play with high pad level, his "speed-to-ball" pursuit and history as a special teams standout make him a high-upside prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft.

name: Jeadyn Lukus
summary: Jeadyn Lukus (6’2/200) represents a "physically dominant" boundary corner who anchored Clemson’s secondary with 61 career tackles and 11 pass breakups. Despite battling injuries throughout his career, Lukus utilized his 2025 campaign to showcase elite "press-man" utility, utilizing his 6’2 frame and "patient hands" to disrupt vertical timing. While his diagnostic processing in zone remains a developmental project, his "alpha" mentality and ball-tracking skills against elite ACC wideouts impressed evaluators during the recent showcase circuit. Currently projected as a mid-round value, Lukus offers the rare length and recovery speed needed to neutralize physical NFL targets. He is a high-floor corner prospect with the competitive grit and frame to start immediately in a professional secondary.

name: Tommy Castellanos
summary: Tommy Castellanos (5’11/201) solidified his status as a "human highlight reel" after a productive 2025 campaign at Florida State following his transfer from Boston College. Recording 2,760 passing yards and 15 touchdowns alongside 557 rushing yards, he excels in "scramble-drill" scenarios where his elite lateral twitch erases pursuit angles. While scouts remain wary of his 58.3% completion rate and a tendency to throw from unoptimized arm angles, his record-breaking 22.1 MPH play speed makes him a defensive coordinator’s nightmare. Currently trending as a late-round developmental project, Castellanos offers the rare high-ceiling mobility required for a "gadget" NFL role or a specialized change-of-pace quarterback. He is a premier high-risk, high-reward playmaker for the 2026 draft cycle.

name: Wesley Bailey
summary: Wesley Bailey is a physically gifted 6'5", 265-pound edge rusher who completed a strong 2025 season at Louisville after transferring from Rutgers. During his lone campaign with the Cardinals, he recorded 39 total tackles, 7 tackles for loss, and 6 sacks, consistently pressuring quarterbacks with a 21.3% pressure rate. Scouts value his NFL-ready frame and elite "speed-to-power" transition, noting his ability to stonewall pulling offensive linemen in the run game. While he sometimes plays with high pad level, his high motor and effective use of arm length allow him to disrupt the backfield and navigate blocks effectively. A former Shrine Bowl 1000 selection, Bailey's blend of length and positional intelligence makes him a rising Day 2 or Day 3 prospect for the 2026 NFL Draft.

name: Joey Aguilar
summary: Joey Aguilar (6’3/215 est.) served as Tennessee’s backup quarterback in 2025, completing passes with good arm strength, quick release, and mobility to extend plays in limited action. Dual-threat traits and accuracy on timing routes appeared in spot duty. Developmental quarterback with backup potential. Decision-making under pressure and pre-snap processing remain inconsistent; happy feet and hold-the-ball tendencies surface against blitzes; arm talent is solid but not elite. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as developmental backup if mechanics and reads are refined.

name: Raion Strader
summary: Raion Strader (6’0/185) returned to his roots in 2026, committing to Pittsburgh as a high-profile transfer after a "sticky" 2025 campaign at Auburn. This local Penn Hills standout excels in press-man coverage, utilizing "patient hands" and elite hip fluidity to record over 10 pass deflections against elite SEC vertical threats. While he must continue adding functional mass to consistently stack-and-shed physical NFL "power" receivers, his 4.45-speed and "ball-hawk" mentality highlight a high-end starter's floor. Following a productive 2025 season where he allowed a career-low passer rating when targeted, Strader is firmly in the Day 2 conversation. He offers the rare combination of length, diagnostic instincts, and recovery burst required to anchor a modern professional secondary.

name: Nic Anderson
summary: Nic Anderson is a 6'4", 208-lb wide receiver who recently transferred to Kentucky after stints at Oklahoma and LSU. A prototypical "X" receiver, Anderson rose to prominence in 2023 at Oklahoma, where he set a freshman record with 10 touchdown receptions and averaged a staggering 21.0 yards per catch. He is a vertical threat who utilizes his massive frame and long strides to "take the top off" defenses. Scouts laud his 50/50 ball ability and catch radius, though he has struggled with consistency and health following an injury-plagued 2024. While he lacks elite short-area quickness, his combination of size and straight-line speed makes him a high-upside red zone and deep-ball specialist for the 2026 NFL Draft.

name: Andre Fuller
summary: Andre Fuller is a long, versatile cornerback who emerged as a premier defender in the MAC after rebounding from an injury-shortened 2024 season. Measuring 6'2" and 202 lbs, Fuller’s primary strengths are his elite length and football IQ, traits honed during his transition from safety to corner. In 2025, he anchored Toledo’s secondary with 49 tackles and 11 passes defended, earning First-Team All-MAC honors. While his size allows him to match up well against larger receivers, scouts have noted his lack of elite top-end speed as a potential weakness against NFL vertical threats. Currently projected as a Day 3 selection (Round 6), his draft stock is rising as a high-upside developmental sleeper.

name: Jackson Kuwatch
summary: Jackson Kuwatch (6’4/250 est.) rotated on Miami (OH)’s edge in 2025, adding tackles, TFLs, and pressures with quick first step and power bull rush. Versatile alignment options and high motor showed in pass-rush and run-defense snaps. Developmental edge with starter traits if strength and bend improve. Bend and corner speed remain average vs. quicker tackles; pass-rush plan is power-heavy with limited counters; leverage issues occur when pad level rises. Mid-to-late Day 3 projection (Round 5-7) in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as rotational disruptor in schemes that value motor and power.

name: Peyton Bowen
summary: Peyton Bowen (6’0/201) operates as a high-IQ "centerfield" safety who utilized a productive 2025 junior campaign to cement his status as a premier post-defender. This former five-star recruit combines elite 4.4-speed with "telepathic" diagnostic instincts, recording over 70 career tackles and multiple special-teams blocks. While he must continue adding functional mass to consistently reroute physical NFL tight ends, his range and "click-and-close" agility make him a defensive coordinator’s dream. Currently trending as a Day 2 selection, Bowen offers the rare versatility to play single-high or drop into the nickel. He is a franchise-altering secondary general.

name: Jake Retzlaff
summary: Jake Retzlaff (6’1/205) emerged as the "comeback story" of 2025, leading Tulane to a 10-win season after a high-profile transfer from BYU. This "gunslinger-style" commander operates with an aggressive, vertical-first mentality, showcasing the arm talent to drive the ball into intermediate windows under heavy pressure. While his "risk-taker" approach leads to occasional turnover-worthy plays, his 22 touchdowns and elite "off-platform" creativity have caught the attention of NFL evaluators. Following a refined Senior Bowl showcasing improved decision-making, Retzlaff projects as a mid-round value. He possesses the raw "cool" and grit required for a professional backup role.

name: Jerry Wilson
summary: Jerry Wilson (5’11/185 est.) contributed in a secondary rotation during 2025, displaying quick feet, competitive ball skills, and solid run support in nickel and boundary snaps. Press technique at the line, mirroring underneath, and instincts in zone/match coverage showed promise. Developmental corner with nickel/slot upside. Recovery speed and burst lag against vertical threats; tackling angles in open space remain inconsistent; technique lapses occur during transitions. Day 3/UDFA projection in the 2026 NFL Draft with upside as depth corner or special teams contributor if strength is added and coverage details are refined.

name: Trey Smack
summary: Trey Smack (6’1/201) stands as the premiere specialist of the 2026 class, having solidified his "deadly" accuracy and range during a standout 2025 season at Florida. Recording multiple 50-plus yard field goals—including a career-long 56-yarder—Smack combines a "piston-like" leg with elite mental composure in high-leverage situations. While most kickers struggle with the jump to NFL-style pressure, his "kickoff-specialist" background and 82% career field-goal rate suggest a Day 1 pro floor. After a "perfect" week in Mobile where he routinely hit from 60 yards in practice, Smack is trending toward a rare fourth-round selection. He is a "franchise-leg" prospect with the leg-strength to erase field-position disadvantages and win games from the logo.

name: Kyron Drones
summary: Kyron Drones is a 6'2", 235-pound dual-threat quarterback from Virginia Tech whose draft stock has cooled following a statistically inconsistent 2025 senior campaign.
